- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Performance & Caching

Logan Wright: TODO: I still need `rbac_mode` label from gateway + `authz_decision_mismatch_total` from policy lib to light up the comparison panels.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: thanks. once those land, we can start using this as the “go/no-go” page for canary flips.

Leila Farouk: once you’ve got the mismatch panel live, ping me. i’ll wire an automated smoke that hits key endpoints and we can watch mismatch/denies on staging before prod.

Allison Grant: also tagging SecOps once alerts are in place. we should have an oncall playbook entry: “if mismatch spike, rollback to dual_read / enforce off” w/ exact flag commands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, i’ll put the exact `rbacctl` commands in runbook + link dashboards.
text:
Connor O'Brien: we need to get ahead of rollout dashboards for RBAC v2 flags before we start canarying in prod. can we align on *what* we’re measuring + who owns panels/alerts?

Connor O'Brien: main flags in play (as of current branch):
- `rbac_v2.dual_read` (evaluate v1 + v2, enforce v1, emit compare metrics)
- `rbac_v2.enforce_gateway`
- `rbac_v2.enforce_services`
- `rbac_v2.debug_denial_headers` (admin-only)

Connor O'Brien: goals for dashboards: deny rate, latency impact, mismatch rate (v1 vs v2), and “coverage gaps” (unannotated endpoints / unknown permissions). need views per env (staging/prod) + per deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).

Logan Wright: +1. I can own the metrics plumbing / grafana dashboard skeleton. need names/labels nailed down so we don’t end up with 12 almost-identical queries.

Allison Grant: for alerts, we should keep it minimal but high-signal. deny spikes + authz eval latency + errors in evaluator client.

Amara Diallo: gateway side: we already emit `http_requests_total{status}` etc but authz needs its own to avoid mixing legitimate 403s with other 403s.

Amara Diallo: proposing gateway metrics (prom):
- `authz_check_total{component="gateway", decision="allow|deny", reason_code, permission, scope_type, env}`
- `authz_check_latency_ms_bucket{component="gateway", env}`
- `authz_middleware_fail_open_total{component="gateway", cause}` (should be 0; but if we ever do safe fallback we need a counter)

Jordan Lee: on mismatch rate in dual_read: we should emit *both* the raw decision and the diff.

Jordan Lee: ex:
- `authz_decision_total{mode="v1|v2", decision="allow|deny", component, env}`
- `authz_decision_mismatch_total{component, env, mismatch_type="v1_allow_v2_deny|v1_deny_v2_allow"}`

Jordan Lee: mismatch_type is critical for rollout. v1_deny_v2_allow is “probably ok” (v2 less strict) but v1_allow_v2_deny is the scary one.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: for services, can we include `service` label? otherwise it’s hard to isolate Control Plane vs console backend etc.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also need a way to track “missing annotation” vs “explicit deny”. we’ve seen both show up as 403 at the edge.

Ethan Park: control plane can emit:
- `authz_enforcement_total{service="control-plane", outcome="allowed|denied|error", denial_reason="policy|missing_permission_annotation|invalid_scope"}`
- `authz_annotation_missing_total{service, route}` (this is the one i want a CI gate on eventually)

Leila Farouk: for rollout criteria, can we define thresholds now? otherwise people will stare at dashboards w/out knowing what “good” looks like.

Connor O'Brien: yes pls. needs to be simple + written into rollout runbook. thinking:
- p95 gateway added latency from authz < 5ms (p99 < 15ms)
- mismatch (v1_allow_v2_deny) < 0.05% of authz checks in canary slice
- error rate in evaluator client < 0.01%
- deny rate: we track but not “must be low” globally, since denies are workload-dependent. what we care about is *delta* post-enable.

Allison Grant: +1 to delta-based deny. alert on “deny rate increased by X%” over baseline per route or per permission.

Allison Grant: but we need a baseline window. recommend:
- compare last 10m vs 1h rolling avg (or same time previous day) for canary cohort.

Logan Wright: cohorting is the hard part. do we have a label for canary vs non-canary? if not, we can do it by `flag_state` label on metrics.

Jordan Lee: we can add `flag_state="dual_read|enforce"` label when emitting metrics from middleware/library. it’ll help a lot.

Amara Diallo: gateway can do that. basically tag metrics with `rbac_mode` based on which flag branch executed.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + need `policy_version` label? at least for audit logs, but for metrics too it’s useful to correlate rollout with policy bundle version.

Jordan Lee: careful w/ cardinality. `policy_version` as a label can explode if it’s a git sha. maybe we do a limited `policy_release="2025-02-23.1"` style.

Logan Wright: agree. let’s use `policy_release` (semver-like) not sha.

Priya Natarajan: stepping in: whatever we ship, keep metrics cardinality sane. do NOT label on `user_id` / `org_id`. permission label is already a lot.

Priya Natarajan: can we sample permission label? e.g., keep per-permission in logs, but metrics roll up permission to `permission_family` (like `controlplane.roles.*`).

Amara Diallo: on gateway, permission strings are high-cardinality but bounded-ish. still, we have ~200-400 perms now and growing. might be ok but we should sanity check.

Allison Grant: bounded perms are fine. it’s `route` that gets messy. suggest:
- metric labels: `permission`, `decision`, `reason_code`, `component`, `service`, `env`, `rbac_mode`
- keep `route` only on a separate metric used for coverage/annotation missing (and we gate on it)

Ethan Park: works for me. for “missing annotation” we can label on `route_template` (e.g., `/v1/orgs/:orgId/roles`) not full path.

Leila Farouk: can we add an explicit panel for “top denial reason codes”? support will ask for this day 1.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, and “top permissions by deny volume” too. plus “top permissions by mismatch volume” during dual_read.

Connor O'Brien: ok dashboard sections i’m hearing:
1) Rollout Overview (by env): flag adoption (dual_read/enforce), total checks, allow/deny, errors
2) Safety: mismatch counts/rate (highlight v1_allow_v2_deny)
3) Performance: authz latency p50/p95/p99, cache hit rate
4) Coverage: missing annotation, unknown permission registry errors
5) Denials: top reason codes, top permissions by denies

Logan Wright: cache metrics from evaluator client:
- `authz_cache_hit_total{cache="decision", component, env}`
- `authz_cache_miss_total{...}`
- `authz_cache_stale_total{...}` (if we detect invalidation race)

Jordan Lee: + `authz_policy_fetch_latency_ms` and `authz_policy_fetch_errors_total` (if client calls policy service / bundle fetch)

Amara Diallo: where are we putting dashboards? Grafana folder `Platform / AuthZ`?

Allison Grant: `Security / RBAC v2 Rollout` might be more discoverable for SecOps + GRC too.

Priya Natarajan: +1 Security folder, but Platform needs edit rights.

Logan Wright: I’ll create `Security/RBAC v2` folder and add:
- `RBAC v2 - Rollout Overview`
- `RBAC v2 - Dual Read Comparison`
- `RBAC v2 - Performance & Caching`

Connor O'Brien: alerts proposal (please poke holes):
- ALERT A: `authz_decision_mismatch_total{mismatch_type="v1_allow_v2_deny"}` rate > 0.1% for 5m in prod canary
- ALERT B: `authz_check_latency_ms` p99 > 20ms for 10m
- ALERT C: `authz_enforcement_total{outcome="error"}` > 0.01% of checks
- ALERT D: `authz_annotation_missing_total` > 0 (in prod) after we flip enforce_services

Ethan Park: ALERT D should be “> 0 in last 10m” once enforce is on. before that it’ll be noisy while we annotate.

Amara Diallo: for gateway latency, separate out “middleware time” from total request latency if possible. otherwise the alert will fire due to unrelated downstream slowness.

Logan Wright: we already measure middleware phase timing in gateway. i’ll plumb it into `authz_check_latency_ms` directly.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: what about “deny rate jump” alert? ex customer gets locked out due to mapping bug.

Allison Grant: I’d avoid global deny rate alert, but we can do “new deny reason codes” / “denies due to missing binding” spiking.

Jordan Lee: we have reason codes from the new deny struct. suggestion:
- alert on `reason_code="NO_BINDING"` and `reason_code="SCOPE_MISMATCH"` spikes specifically.

Connor O'Brien: ok. so alert E:
- `authz_check_total{decision="deny", reason_code=~"NO_BINDING|SCOPE_MISMATCH"}` increase > 3x baseline (15m vs 2h) in prod canary.

Leila Farouk: rollout stages — can we put them in the dashboard as an annotation? like vertical lines when we flipped flags.

Logan Wright: yes. if we emit a deploy event / flag flip event to Grafana annotations. can be manual but better if automated.

DeployBot: Deployment started: `control-plane@rbac-v2-metrics-annot` to environment `staging` (initiated by Logan Wright)

Amara Diallo: staged enablement plan reminder (so dashboard filters match):
- staging: dual_read on 100%
- prod: dual_read 5% canary for 24h -> 25% -> 100%
- then enforce_gateway 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100%
- then enforce_services behind service-specific allowlist (control-plane first)

Ethan Park: for Dedicated/Private: we should default to dual_read only until policy bundle has the dedicated/private role templates shipped. otherwise we’ll see NO_BINDING storms.

Connor O'Brien: yep. i’ll add “deployment_mode” filter. dedicated/private will start later with pilot accounts.

Priya Natarajan: action items:
- Logan: create Grafana dashboards + wire required metrics names/labels (today/tomorrow)
- Amara: ensure gateway emits rbac_mode + reason_code labels + middleware latency (this week)
- Ethan/Kaitlyn: service-side missing annotation counters + reason_code parity (this week)
- Connor: update rollout runbook w/ thresholds + alerting + stage plan (by EOW)

Logan Wright: works. I’ll post links once dashboards are up.

Logan Wright: one more: do we want a “403 breakdown panel” per route? support tends to ask “is this authz or something else”.

Allison Grant: maybe a small panel: `http_403_total` split by `error_type` where `error_type="authz_denied"` vs `error_type="other"`. if we can tag responses.

Amara Diallo: gateway already sets `x-redwood-error-code`. we can map it in metrics as `error_code` (bounded). i’ll add `error_family` = `authz` for the new RBAC denies.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. please make sure debug header is only for admin + when `rbac_v2.debug_denial_headers` flag is on. we don’t want to leak internals.

Jordan Lee: agree. debug headers should include reason_code + required permission + evaluated scope, but only in admin mode.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: “why denied” UX depends on those reason codes. let’s keep the list stable.

Logan Wright: ok logging note: for audit/evidence, metrics aren’t enough. but for rollout, dashboards + alerts should keep us safe.

DeployBot: Deployment finished: `control-plane@rbac-v2-metrics-annot` to environment `staging` (duration: 7m12s)

Logan Wright: dashboards created (staging data only for now):
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Rollout Overview
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Dual Read Comparison
- Grafana: Security/RBAC v2/Performance & Caching

Logan Wright: TODO: I still need `rbac_mode` label from gateway + `authz_decision_mismatch_total` from policy lib to light up the comparison panels.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: thanks. once those land, we can start using this as the “go/no-go” page for canary flips.

Leila Farouk: once you’ve got the mismatch panel live, ping me. i’ll wire an automated smoke that hits key endpoints and we can watch mismatch/denies on staging before prod.

Allison Grant: also tagging SecOps once alerts are in place. we should have an oncall playbook entry: “if mismatch spike, rollback to dual_read / enforce off” w/ exact flag commands.

Connor O'Brien: yep, i’ll put the exact `rbacctl` commands in runbook + link dashboards.

source.content:
Allison Grant: Dropping early cache dashboards here for eyes 👀 https://grafana.redwood-internal/p/dash-cache-telemetry-v0 (behind VPN). Looking for feedback on panels + filters before we bake alerts. Current top-level filters: `deployment_mode`, `model`, `route_id` (allowlisted), `region` (limited set). Anything missing / any panels confusing?

Sergio Costa: skimmed. Overall solid. A few notes:
- I really want a model-family breakdown (e.g. llama / mistral / qwen) in addition to specific model_version. Otherwise comparisons are noisy when versions churn.
- region breakdown needs to be front + center, not buried. Most cache regressions we’ve seen are locality/placement.
- prompt length buckets: can we get a consistent `prompt_len_bucket` tag on the hist + the hit-rate panel? Right now I only see it on the reuse depth histogram.

Logan Wright: +1 on prompt length bucket. The “overall hit-rate” graph is hard to interpret without the request shape mix.
Also: route_id filter is useful but we have to be careful w/ cardinality. Are we already aggregating to route_group for hosted?

Nadia Rahman: re: model family — we can add a derived label in dashboards (regex on `model_id`) but would prefer runtime/gateway to emit an explicit `model_family` from the allowlist so we don’t end up with 15 different regex variants across dashboards.
On prompt buckets: standards doc proposes: `prompt_len_bucket` = {"0-256","257-512","513-1k","1k-2k","2k-4k","4k-8k","8k+"} and we should reuse across all cache metrics.

Ethan Park: region should be safe as a tag (small set). model_family should be safe too if allowlisted.
route_id: we’re sending `route` as a stable id, but for hosted we also have `route_group` which collapses into like ~200 groups (vs 10Ks routes). I’d suggest dashboard default uses `route_group` and keeps `route` behind a “high-cardinality” toggle.

Paula Mendes: from PM side (telemetry requirements):
- need a “by deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)” view and then drill into region.
- for customer comms we’ll want a single panel that answers: “is caching helping?” and “did it regress?” — so like a KPI tile for `cache_hit_rate` + `wasted_prefill_rate` with week-over-week delta.
- prompt-length bucket is important for Optimize recommendations; if we can’t slice by request shape, we can’t safely suggest max_prefix_cache_bytes changes.

Aisha Bello: +1 to Sergio. The miss reason panel is good but I’m missing a “region comparison” view for the same model/route.
Something like small multiples: columns = region, lines = hit rate over time. That’s the fastest way to spot a single region being off.
Also: can we label the miss reasons in human terms? `hash_mismatch` vs `prompt_hash_mismatch` has been confusing in past dashboards.

Allison Grant: super helpful. On prompt_len_bucket: we intentionally didn’t add it to everything yet due to series count fears, but sounds like it’s required to make the panels actionable.
Question to Telemetry folks: if we add `prompt_len_bucket` to the *hit-rate numerator/denominator* counters, are we still within budget w/ region+model+deployment?

Caleb Johnson: quick napkin math: if we do {deployment_mode (3)} x {region (~8)} x {model_family (~6)} x {prompt_len_bucket (~7)} that’s 1008 series *per metric* before route.
That’s fine for core metrics if we keep route to `route_group` (<=200) and only allow full `route` in sampled metrics or via logs/traces.
In ingestion we can also enforce: if `route` is present, drop `prompt_len_bucket` (or vice versa) depending on the metric. We already have the label guardrails ready in the ingestion PR.

Logan Wright: +1 to “route xor prompt bucket” rule for counters. In practice when triaging, I either want:
A) per-route view (aggregate request shapes), OR
B) per-shape view (aggregate routes)
Not both at once.

Nadia Rahman: we should document that behavior clearly in the dashboard. Otherwise people will think data is missing.
Also I’d prefer we standardize label names now: `model_family`, `region`, `deployment_mode`, `route_group`, `prompt_len_bucket`.
If we keep `model_version` it should be optional + allowlisted (or only in analytics aggregates).

Sergio Costa: On panels themselves:
- “KV cache reuse effectiveness” graph: can we add a second axis or companion panel for `eviction_pressure`? otherwise it’s hard to tell whether reuse dropped due to churn vs policy.
- “Wasted prefill” panel: can we express it as a ratio (wasted_prefill_tokens / total_prefill_tokens) + absolute tokens. Ratios hide volume changes.

Felix Schneider: +1 on model family. In console / internal report we already talk in “model family” language, and operators don’t want to mentally map `llama3.1-70b-instruct-v3.2` to “llama3”.
If we can get `model_family` as a tag, it’ll align with the reporting tables too.

Allison Grant: ok, action items I’m hearing:
1) Make region + model_family a first-class breakdown (top row / default view).
2) Add prompt_len_bucket to the core hit/miss counters, but enforce cardinality rules (route_group default, avoid route+bucket combo).
3) Add small multiples for region comparison.
4) Improve wasted prefill visualization (ratio + absolute).
5) Rename/alias miss reasons to human labels in the dashboard.

Allison Grant: Logan/Nadia/Caleb — can you confirm the exact label guardrail so I can annotate the dashboard and not surprise anyone?

Caleb Johnson: proposal:
- For metrics with `route` allowed: only permit `route_group` + drop `prompt_len_bucket`.
- For metrics with `prompt_len_bucket`: only permit `route_group` (no `route`).
- `tenant_id` never allowed on these metrics.
- `model_version` only allowed on sampled metrics (or analytics rollups), dashboards default to `model`+`model_family`.
We can encode this in ingestion rules + runtime allowlist.

Nadia Rahman: works for me. I’ll update the schema doc + ping Omar/Samir so runtime emits `model_family` and `prompt_len_bucket` consistently.

Paula Mendes: please also add a quick note in the dashboard description: “prompt_len_bucket uses prefill prompt tokens after canonicalization; no raw prompts captured.” Keeps us aligned on privacy.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: will incorporate and push a v1 update today + repost link. If anyone has strong opinions on the bucket boundaries, drop them here before we lock.

Sergio Costa: buckets Nadia listed are fine. Only request: keep `8k+` as a single bucket so we don’t explode series on long-context traffic.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also can we add a “workload mix” panel: stacked area of request counts by prompt_len_bucket? That’ll help explain hit-rate shifts.

Allison Grant: yes, adding “request volume by prompt_len_bucket” stacked area + annotate with major deploys/rollouts.

Logan Wright: last nit: can we move “policy_disabled” misses into its own panel or at least call it out. It’s a different class of issue than “capacity/eviction”.

Allison Grant: good call. I’ll split miss reasons into “config/policy” vs “runtime/cache pressure” sections.

Allison Grant: closing loop: I’ll post updated link + tag @sean (SRE) once it’s ready for alert review.

Ethan Park: heads up — we’re seeing early signs of a thundering herd risk on model-registry lookups now that quant_profile resolution is in the policy path. If we have a cold cache / deploy, every router instance will do `GET /models/:id/profiles` at once under high QPS.

Mina Haddad: yep, esp. dedicated clusters where autoscale adds 10–20 pods and they all warm at the same time.

Ravi Menon: +1. we should treat registry as control plane, not something we hit per request. what’s our current caching story in policy-eval?

Benji Okafor: right now: policy engine does an in-process cache for resolved route config (tenant/route), but the quant profile metadata fetch is still “on miss => call registry”. TTL is like 30s for route config, but profile metadata isn’t cached separately.

Jordan Lee: determinism angle: we can cache, but we need to be careful about “recommended/latest” semantics. if profile ref can move, we don’t want pods disagreeing.

Ethan Park: agreed. even if we ban aliases for v1 (ADR trend), we’ll still cache profile metadata by immutable `profile_id` / version.

Michael Nguyen: implementation idea: two-level cache:
1) L1: in-proc LRU (per router/policy) keyed by (model_id, profile_id) w/ TTL + jitter
2) L2: shared cache (redis/memcached) to smooth deploy spikes

Diego Santos: do we already have shared cache infra in that service? i’d prefer not to introduce redis dependency in routing hot path unless we have to.

Benji Okafor: we have the config-service side already using the platform cache (redis) for tenant config reads, but policy-engine runs in the routing service too. Adding redis calls in routing path is… :grimacing:

Mina Haddad: can we get 90% benefit just with L1 + singleflight? like per-process request coalescing so 1 miss triggers 1 fetch, others wait.

Ravi Menon: + singleflight + backoff. if registry starts 429’ing, we should fail closed to baseline (no profile) and not retry storm.

Jordan Lee: +1. I want explicit “stale-while-revalidate” behavior:
- if cached profile metadata is expired but not too old, keep using it for compatibility decisions
- refresh async
- if refresh fails, keep stale for a bounded window

Quentin Harris: from registry side: we can support ETags / If-None-Match to reduce payload but still, the herd is the bigger issue. we’d rather see clients do coalescing + longer TTL.

Ethan Park: what TTL are we comfortable with? profile metadata changes only when Applied ML publishes/deprecates, not minute-to-minute.

Quentin Harris: honestly 10–30 min is fine for metadata, as long as you respect “revoked” / “blocked” flags quickly. we do have deprecation/disabled states.

Connor O'Brien: ops POV: if we need to yank a profile fast (quality regression), we can flip allowlist / kill-switch. but that change must propagate quickly. caching can’t delay kill-switch.

Benji Okafor: kill-switch is a separate config flag from config-service, not registry metadata. we can make sure kill-switch bypasses caches (or has very short TTL).

Mina Haddad: so:
- quant_profile_id metadata cache TTL: 10m (with +/- 20% jitter)
- route config cache TTL: keep 30s
- kill-switch TTL: 5s (or push-based)

Ravi Menon: and add “negative caching” too. if profile doesn’t exist / incompatible -> cache that for short time (e.g. 60s) so we don’t spam registry with bad IDs.

Jordan Lee: careful: incompatible can depend on GPU SKU / max context. that’s local. but “profile not found” is safe for negative caching.

Ethan Park: yeah, we’ll cache *metadata* (includes supported GPU classes, max_context, etc). compatibility check remains local.

Michael Nguyen: singleflight sketch (Go-ish):

```go
v, err := profileCache.Get(key)
if err == nil { return v }

v, err, _ := group.Do(key, func() (any, error) {
  // re-check cache inside singleflight
  if v2, ok := profileCache.Get(key); ok { return v2, nil }
  return registryClient.FetchProfile(ctx, key)
})
```

Benji Okafor: + add a hard cap on concurrent outbound registry requests per pod (semaphore), so worst case we don’t take down our own latency.

Diego Santos: what’s the fallback behavior if registry is down and cache empty? we should default to baseline precision (feature-off behavior) vs error.

Ravi Menon: +1 fallback to baseline + emit metric `quant_profile_resolution=fallback_registry_unavailable`.

Mina Haddad: plus a counter for “registry fetch QPS” so we can alert before it explodes.

Quentin Harris: registry team can also rate-limit by client id. please set a distinct UA / header so we can see routing callers.

Ethan Park: action items:
- Benji: implement L1 cache + singleflight + semaphore in policy engine
- Michael: add config knobs (ttl_ms, max_stale_ms, negative_ttl_ms) + defaults
- Mina/Ravi: add metrics + dashboard panel for registry_fetch_rate + fetch_errors + cache_hit_ratio
- Quentin: confirm best endpoint for metadata-only fetch and ETag support

Jordan Lee: and document determinism: cached profile metadata must not change the applied profile id. selection must be pinned by resolved `profile_id` from config. caching only affects metadata fetch + compatibility checks.

Connor O'Brien: please loop me on rollouts. if you’re changing caching behavior, I want a canary in perf-canary + a quick rollback path (feature flag to disable profile metadata fetch maybe?).

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll add `QUANT_PROFILE_METADATA_CACHE_ENABLED` flag so we can turn it off if we break something.

Ethan Park: cool. let’s aim for a PR by EOW; this is one of the big “don’t page SRE at 2am” items before beta.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup:

Quentin Harris: :+1: (and thank you for not turning registry into a hot path 😅)

source.text:
Ethan Park: Heads up: we’re about to land the readiness gate before nodes can join serving pools (PR 18496). Want to sanity check fallback behavior when capacity is tight. If gate is strict, a bad validation run could block scale-up and we’d be “healthy but empty”. How do we want this to behave?

Benji Okafor: Current implementation is strict by default: node must be `Ready` in readiness service or it won’t get added to serving pool (and stays cordoned). There’s a feature flag to disable the gate per region/pool as a kill switch.

Jordan Lee: +1 strict by default. We have too many post-provision surprises. Let’s not make “kinda-valid” a thing.

Vanessa Ortiz: Strict is correct, but we need a clear escape hatch during incidents. If us-east A100 is short, ops will ask “can we serve on nodes stuck in Validating?”

Keiko Yamamoto: From autoscaling POV: if nodes aren’t admitted, the pool looks undersized, autoscaler will keep requesting more instances. That can amplify cost + thrash if validation is failing consistently.

Miles Thornton: This is basically “admission control”. If validation is failing, admitting nodes to serve will just shift the failure to customer traffic. Better to route elsewhere (other region / compatible model) than serve on unknown nodes.

Ethan Park: Agree in principle, but we should write down the exact fallback sequence.

Rafael Mendes: SRE concern: in a tight capacity event, we need deterministic behavior. I’d rather have explicit policy: 1) keep gate strict, 2) attempt capacity fallback (other pool/region), 3) if still burning, allow manual override with auditing + time limit.

Priya Natarajan: +1. We should not have “automatic bypass when tight” as default. That’s an implicit reliability regression.

Harish Reddy: Practical question: who flips the bypass flag? Infra on-call? Platform on-call? Also needs to be super obvious in the console if bypass is enabled.

Benji Okafor: The current flag is owned by control-plane config (same mechanism as staged rollout flags). We can scope it by `region` and `pool_type` (Dedicated vs shared). We also emit an audit event when toggled (separate PR 18503 is adding audit for image channel changes; we can follow the same pattern here).

Ethan Park: What about allowing a “limited join” state? Like join the cluster but don’t go into serving endpoints?

Jordan Lee: That’s already the behavior. Node can join k8s and run validations, but the serving pool membership controller won’t add it to the pool until `Ready`.

Vanessa Ortiz: Can we make sure it doesn’t accidentally get picked up by the scheduler even if it’s joined? Some teams rely on labels/taints but those drift.

Benji Okafor: The admission gate controls the serving pool membership (our control-plane “serving allocator”), but we also keep the node tainted until `Ready` to reduce accidental workload scheduling. Double lock.

Keiko Yamamoto: On autoscaler thrash: can we stop scaling when readiness failures spike? Like “if 80% of nodes fail validation in last 30m, pause scale-up and page”? Otherwise we’ll keep adding nodes that never go Ready.

Miles Thornton: That fits with error budget policy. If we’re failing validation, that’s not a demand issue, it’s a supply quality issue.

Rafael Mendes: We can do: if `validation_failure_rate > X` OR `p95_time_to_ready > Y` then disable further expansion automatically (or at least degrade to tickets). But auto-pausing scale has risk too.

Priya Natarajan: Let’s keep it simple for sprint: strict gate + manual bypass + routing fallback. Auto-pause scale-up is a follow-up once we’ve seen data.

Ethan Park: Ok, so fallback behavior when a pool is short:
1) Pool cannot admit non-Ready nodes.
2) Router should attempt alternate capacity tier/region/model per existing fallback policies.
3) If still insufficient, on-call can temporarily disable readiness gate for that pool/region (explicit, logged, time-boxed).

Harish Reddy: Time-boxed is important. Seen flags get left on for weeks.

Benji Okafor: We can add a TTL in config? Not sure our flag system supports TTL natively.

Connor O'Brien: Release process wise: if we ship a bypass without TTL, we should at least add a checklist item in incident template + release notes: “verify gate re-enabled”. Also, we can add an alert if gate disabled > 2h.

Rafael Mendes: +1 alert. Paging might be too noisy, but at least a Slack channel notification + ticket.

Miles Thornton: Also want to avoid “silent degraded”. Add a metric tag `readiness_gate=disabled` on serving allocator decisions so dashboards show when we’re bypassing.

Ethan Park: Question: should bypass admit *all* nodes, or only those that passed some subset of checks?

Vanessa Ortiz: If we’re bypassing, it’s usually because one check is flaky (NCCL timeout, perf canary too strict). GPU health + driver parity should still be enforced.

Jordan Lee: That implies a second policy mode: “partial gate”. That’s more complexity than I’d like right now.

Benji Okafor: The flag right now is binary: gate enforced vs not. If we want “partial”, it needs work in readiness service / allocator. Might be post-sprint.

Priya Natarajan: Keep binary. If we bypass, we accept risk and we do it explicitly.

Keiko Yamamoto: Can we at least document the recommended decision tree? Like: if capacity shortfall, first reduce quarantine thresholds? (kidding) but seriously: prefer re-run validations / reimage vs bypass.

Rafael Mendes: Yes. Runbook should say:
- If nodes stuck Validating: check dashboards, retry validation once, then reimage.
- If widespread: rollback image channel candidate->stable.
- Only if customer impact is ongoing and we can’t route elsewhere: bypass gate.

Ethan Park: Great. I’ll add notes to the PR description and ping docs owner to reflect in internal ops doc.

Benji Okafor: One more implementation detail: allocator currently checks readiness state via readiness svc cached read. If readiness svc is down, allocator fails closed (treat as not Ready). Is that acceptable?

Miles Thornton: Fail closed is safer but could cause an outage if readiness svc blips.

Priya Natarajan: For now yes, fail closed. But we should ensure readiness svc is HA + has a clear SLO. If it’s a new dependency on serving, it needs to be rock solid.

Rafael Mendes: Add an alert: readiness svc error rate + allocator “blocked_by_readiness_service” counters. If we see service unavail driving capacity loss, we’ll revisit.

Ethan Park: Action items:
- Add alert/notification if readiness gate disabled > 2h (Connor/Rafael?)
- Add metric tag for allocator decisions when gate disabled (Benji)
- Document fallback sequence + on-call decision tree (Vanessa/Harish coordinate w/ runbook)
- Track follow-up: “partial gate / minimum checks” as backlog (Ethan)

Harish Reddy: +1. Also please make sure internal console shows gate status per pool (even just a banner). Otherwise ops will be guessing.

Benji Okafor: Will ping DES-301 folks; we can add a small field in readiness summary endpoint later. For now, I can add it to the internal endpoint output.

Ethan Park: Cool. Proceeding with strict gate default + kill switch + routed fallback. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1740215540
preview:
Ethan Park: Heads up: we’re about to land the readiness gate before nodes can join serving pools (PR 18496). Want to sanity check fallback behavior when capacity is tight. If gate is strict, a bad validation run could block scale-up and we’d be “healthy but empty”. How do we want this to behave?

Benji Okafor: Current implementation is strict by default: node must be `Ready` in readiness service or it won’t get added to serving pool (and stays cordoned). There’s a feature flag to disable the gate per region/pool as a kill switch.

Jordan Lee: +1 strict by default. We have too many post-provision surprises. Let’s not make “kinda-valid” a thing.

Vanessa Ortiz: Strict is correct, but we need a clear escape hatch during incidents. If us-east A100 is short, ops will ask “can we serve on nodes stuck in Validating?”

Keiko Yamamoto: From autoscaling POV: if nodes aren’t admitted, the pool looks undersized, autoscaler will keep requesting more instances. That can amplify cost + thrash if validation is failing consistently.

Miles Thornton: This is basically “admission control”. If validation is failing, admitting nodes to serve will just shift the failure to customer traffic. Better to route elsewhere (other region / compatible model) than serve on unknown nodes.

Ethan Park: Agree in principle, but we should write down the exact fallback sequence.

Rafael Mendes: SRE concern: in a tight capacity event, we need deterministic behavior. I’d rather have explicit policy: 1) keep gate strict, 2) attempt capacity fallback (other pool/region), 3) if still burning, allow manual override with auditing + time limit.

Priya Natarajan: +1. We should not have “automatic bypass when tight” as default. That’s an implicit reliability regression.

Harish Reddy: Practical question: who flips the bypass flag? Infra on-call? Platform on-call? Also needs to be super obvious in the console if bypass is enabled.

Benji Okafor: The current flag is owned by control-plane config (same mechanism as staged rollout flags). We can scope it by `region` and `pool_type` (Dedicated vs shared). We also emit an audit event when toggled (separate PR 18503 is adding audit for image channel changes; we can follow the same pattern here).

Ethan Park: What about allowing a “limited join” state? Like join the cluster but don’t go into serving endpoints?

Jordan Lee: That’s already the behavior. Node can join k8s and run validations, but the serving pool membership controller won’t add it to the pool until `Ready`.

Vanessa Ortiz: Can we make sure it doesn’t accidentally get picked up by the scheduler even if it’s joined? Some teams rely on labels/taints but those drift.

Benji Okafor: The admission gate controls the serving pool membership (our control-plane “serving allocator”), but we also keep the node tainted until `Ready` to reduce accidental workload scheduling. Double lock.

Keiko Yamamoto: On autoscaler thrash: can we stop scaling when readiness failures spike? Like “if 80% of nodes fail validation in last 30m, pause scale-up and page”? Otherwise we’ll keep adding nodes that never go Ready.

Miles Thornton: That fits with error budget policy. If we’re failing validation, that’s not a demand issue, it’s a supply quality issue.

Rafael Mendes: We can do: if `validation_failure_rate > X` OR `p95_time_to_ready > Y` then disable further expansion automatically (or at least degrade to tickets). But auto-pausing scale has risk too.

Priya Natarajan: Let’s keep it simple for sprint: strict gate + manual bypass + routing fallback. Auto-pause scale-up is a follow-up once we’ve seen data.

Ethan Park: Ok, so fallback behavior when a pool is short:
1) Pool cannot admit non-Ready nodes.
2) Router should attempt alternate capacity tier/region/model per existing fallback policies.
3) If still insufficient, on-call can temporarily disable readiness gate for that pool/region (explicit, logged, time-boxed).

Harish Reddy: Time-boxed is important. Seen flags get left on for weeks.

Benji Okafor: We can add a TTL in config? Not sure our flag system supports TTL natively.

Connor O'Brien: Release process wise: if we ship a bypass without TTL, we should at least add a checklist item in incident template + release notes: “verify gate re-enabled”. Also, we can add an alert if gate disabled > 2h.

Rafael Mendes: +1 alert. Paging might be too noisy, but at least a Slack channel notification + ticket.

Miles Thornton: Also want to avoid “silent degraded”. Add a metric tag `readiness_gate=disabled` on serving allocator decisions so dashboards show when we’re bypassing.

Ethan Park: Question: should bypass admit *all* nodes, or only those that passed some subset of checks?

Vanessa Ortiz: If we’re bypassing, it’s usually because one check is flaky (NCCL timeout, perf canary too strict). GPU health + driver parity should still be enforced.

Jordan Lee: That implies a second policy mode: “partial gate”. That’s more complexity than I’d like right now.

Benji Okafor: The flag right now is binary: gate enforced vs not. If we want “partial”, it needs work in readiness service / allocator. Might be post-sprint.

Priya Natarajan: Keep binary. If we bypass, we accept risk and we do it explicitly.

Keiko Yamamoto: Can we at least document the recommended decision tree? Like: if capacity shortfall, first reduce quarantine thresholds? (kidding) but seriously: prefer re-run validations / reimage vs bypass.

Rafael Mendes: Yes. Runbook should say:
- If nodes stuck Validating: check dashboards, retry validation once, then reimage.
- If widespread: rollback image channel candidate->stable.
- Only if customer impact is ongoing and we can’t route elsewhere: bypass gate.

Ethan Park: Great. I’ll add notes to the PR description and ping docs owner to reflect in internal ops doc.

Benji Okafor: One more implementation detail: allocator currently checks readiness state via readiness svc cached read. If readiness svc is down, allocator fails closed (treat as not Ready). Is that acceptable?

Miles Thornton: Fail closed is safer but could cause an outage if readiness svc blips.

Priya Natarajan: For now yes, fail closed. But we should ensure readiness svc is HA + has a clear SLO. If it’s a new dependency on serving, it needs to be rock solid.

Rafael Mendes: Add an alert: readiness svc error rate + allocator “blocked_by_readiness_service” counters. If we see service unavail driving capacity loss, we’ll revisit.

Ethan Park: Action items:
- Add alert/notification if readiness gate disabled > 2h (Connor/Rafael?)
- Add metric tag for allocator decisions when gate disabled (Benji)
- Document fallback sequence + on-call decision tree (Vanessa/Harish coordinate w/ runbook)
- Track follow-up: “partial gate / minimum checks” as backlog (Ethan)

Harish Reddy: +1. Also please make sure internal console shows gate status per pool (even just a banner). Otherwise ops will be guessing.

Benji Okafor: Will ping DES-301 folks; we can add a small field in readiness summary endpoint later. For now, I can add it to the internal endpoint output.

Ethan Park: Cool. Proceeding with strict gate default + kill switch + routed fallback. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: need to close the loop on bot OAuth/service identity scopes + audit events before we flip config-diff + privileged telemetry on in prod. Can we align today on:
1) exact scopes incident-bot gets
2) what audit events we emit + required fields
3) break-glass flow for Sev0/Sev1

Ethan Park: +1. also want to make sure the control plane gateway enforces scopes consistently across deploy-metadata/config-diff/telemetry endpoints. we’ve got some drift.

Miles Thornton: incident-bot side: we can plumb whatever token/scopes model y’all land on, but we need something stable for v2 beta. main thing: oncall shouldn’t have to ask for perms mid-incident.

Marcus Lin: security req: least privilege by default, and any access to tenant-scoped config/telemetry must be audit logged w/ actor + purpose. “bot did it” isn’t sufficient.

Kevin Osei: + retention + SIEM routing. we need events to show up in audit index with consistent names. please don’t invent 12 variants.

Benji Okafor: for config diff endpoint (`/v1/config/diff` internal), the thing we need is read-only access to diff *after* redaction hooks run. endpoint is tenant-aware and already checks authz, but we need a clean scope for bot.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can do:
- allow `incident-bot` service principal
- require scope `config.diff.read`
- optionally require `incident_context=true` claim so it can’t be used as a general-purpose diff client

Devon Price: question: is this OAuth as in “Slack user installs app and bot inherits user perms” or is it pure service-to-service with a bot identity?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pure service-to-service for the data plane calls. Slack interaction is user-triggered, but the backend calls are from bot service identity. we’ll include `acting_user` in the audit payload from Slack request context.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side, that’s what we want: bot always works, but we can audit who clicked “Show diff” etc.

Ethan Park: ok so split:
- bot has baseline scopes (deploy context + safe telemetry summaries)
- “expand” actions might require elevated scopes or break-glass?

Marcus Lin: careful: “expand” tends to be where leakage happens. i’d rather keep bot scopes limited and gate any more detailed views behind explicit, audited “break-glass” or per-rotation role.

Miles Thornton: bot UX already has two tiers in the mock:
- card summary (safe)
- button: “View full diff” / “Open telemetry breakdown”
We can make the second path do an extra auth check.

Benji Okafor: config diff output is always redacted (allowlist + secret patterns). but there’s still “sensitive-ish” (customer routing policy, quotas). so yeah still treat as privileged.

Kevin Osei: audit events should capture *what type* of privileged operation happened. ex:
- CONFIG_DIFF_READ
- PRIV_TELEMETRY_QUERY
- BREAK_GLASS_GRANT / BREAK_GLASS_USE
Also include `tenant_id`/`org_id` when applicable, and `incident_id` if present.

Jordan Lee: we can standardize an event schema:
fields:
- event_name
- actor_type (service)
- actor_id (incident-bot)
- acting_user (slack user id/email if we have it)
- reason (e.g., "incident_triage")
- incident_id (from thread/incident command context)
- resource_type (config/metrics)
- resource_id (tenant_id + service + region)
- decision (allow/deny)
- policy_version

Kaitlyn Nguyen: sounds right. for scopes, proposing:
Baseline bot scopes:
- `deploy.metadata.read` (recent deploys card)
- `telemetry.saturation.read` (gpu util/queue depth/kv cache pressure *aggregated only*)
- `slo.burn.read` (error budget burn link gen)
Privileged scopes (only when user clicks expand AND they’re in allowed group / break-glass):
- `config.diff.read`
- `telemetry.tenant_query.read` (tenant-aware query primitives)

Ethan Park: does `telemetry.saturation.read` hit the same query service as tenant-aware queries? want to avoid “baseline scope still lets you do arbitrary queries”

Logan Wright (via Miles, pasted): baseline saturation can be a separate endpoint with fixed query templates + hard-coded label filters. tenant_query is the flexible one. keep them separate.

Devon Price: implementation detail: Slack action -> bot backend -> call authz service “can user X request privileged operation Y for incident Z?”
If allowed, bot uses *same* service token but with a different scope? or do we mint a short-lived token with elevated scope?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: mint short-lived token with elevated scope via internal auth service. scope bound + TTL 5m. include `acting_user` + `incident_id` claims.

Marcus Lin: +1 short-lived. also require that the privileged token includes `purpose=incident` and `channel=#incidents|#eng-oncall` (or at least source context). if someone replays it elsewhere, it should fail.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can enforce claim `purpose=incident_triage` and `incident_id` present for `config.diff.read` and `telemetry.tenant_query.read`.

Benji Okafor: for break-glass: do we pre-provision “OncallIncidentBotPrivileged” group membership, or do we have an explicit “request break-glass” button?

Rafael Mendes: for the beta, simplest is group-based: SRE oncall rotation + platform oncall have it. Longer term, we can do “request break-glass” with approval trail.

Kevin Osei: group-based still needs audit. every privileged read emits event; monthly review pulls from SIEM.

Ethan Park: and what about bot *itself* calling privileged endpoints in degraded mode? e.g., if auth service is down.

Miles Thornton: if authz is down we should fail closed for privileged (show link + “permissions check unavailable”), but still show baseline cards. no “silent allow” during outage.

Marcus Lin: yes. fail closed for privileged.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok, action items:
- Kaitlyn: write up final scopes list + claims requirements and drop in ADR comment (today)
- Jordan: update policy engine rules for `config.diff.read` + `telemetry.tenant_query.read` with required claims + deny-by-default (tomorrow)
- Benji: ensure config diff endpoint emits audit event with schema above and includes redaction version/hash (this week)
- Kevin: confirm event names + SIEM routing + retention reqs, share list of required fields (today)
- Devon/Miles: bot path mints 5m privileged token on “expand” + passes acting_user/incident_id + handles fail-closed (this week)

Kevin Osei: for event naming, propose:
- `audit.incident_bot.config_diff.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.telemetry.tenant_query.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.break_glass.token_issued`
Keep in lower_snake or dotted, but consistent.

Marcus Lin: + include `redaction_profile` and `result_count` (e.g., diff lines) to help spot weird usage patterns.

Devon Price: ack. for acting_user, we can map Slack user -> email via SCIM. if missing, log slack user id.

Ethan Park: please also log `service` + `region` on telemetry queries. otherwise it’s useless in reviews.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: got it. i’ll send a short summary in-thread once the doc is updated.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: once scopes are final, we’ll update the bot env + start staging validation.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post policy diff PR link here when ready.

Ethan Park: and what about bot *itself* calling privileged endpoints in degraded mode? e.g., if auth service is down.

Miles Thornton: if authz is down we should fail closed for privileged (show link + “permissions check unavailable”), but still show baseline cards. no “silent allow” during outage.

Marcus Lin: yes. fail closed for privileged.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok, action items:
- Kaitlyn: write up final scopes list + claims requirements and drop in ADR comment (today)
- Jordan: update policy engine rules for `config.diff.read` + `telemetry.tenant_query.read` with required claims + deny-by-default (tomorrow)
- Benji: ensure config diff endpoint emits audit event with schema above and includes redaction version/hash (this week)
- Kevin: confirm event names + SIEM routing + retention reqs, share list of required fields (today)
- Devon/Miles: bot path mints 5m privileged token on “expand” + passes acting_user/incident_id + handles fail-closed (this week)

Kevin Osei: for event naming, propose:
- `audit.incident_bot.config_diff.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.telemetry.tenant_query.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.break_glass.token_issued`
Keep in lower_snake or dotted, but consistent.

Marcus Lin: + include `redaction_profile` and `result_count` (e.g., diff lines) to help spot weird usage patterns.

Devon Price: ack. for acting_user, we can map Slack user -> email via SCIM. if missing, log slack user id.

Ethan Park: please also log `service` + `region` on telemetry queries. otherwise it’s useless in reviews.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: got it. i’ll send a short summary in-thread once the doc is updated.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: once scopes are final, we’ll update the bot env + start staging validation.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post policy diff PR link here when ready.
text:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: need to close the loop on bot OAuth/service identity scopes + audit events before we flip config-diff + privileged telemetry on in prod. Can we align today on:
1) exact scopes incident-bot gets
2) what audit events we emit + required fields
3) break-glass flow for Sev0/Sev1

Ethan Park: +1. also want to make sure the control plane gateway enforces scopes consistently across deploy-metadata/config-diff/telemetry endpoints. we’ve got some drift.

Miles Thornton: incident-bot side: we can plumb whatever token/scopes model y’all land on, but we need something stable for v2 beta. main thing: oncall shouldn’t have to ask for perms mid-incident.

Marcus Lin: security req: least privilege by default, and any access to tenant-scoped config/telemetry must be audit logged w/ actor + purpose. “bot did it” isn’t sufficient.

Kevin Osei: + retention + SIEM routing. we need events to show up in audit index with consistent names. please don’t invent 12 variants.

Benji Okafor: for config diff endpoint (`/v1/config/diff` internal), the thing we need is read-only access to diff *after* redaction hooks run. endpoint is tenant-aware and already checks authz, but we need a clean scope for bot.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can do:
- allow `incident-bot` service principal
- require scope `config.diff.read`
- optionally require `incident_context=true` claim so it can’t be used as a general-purpose diff client

Devon Price: question: is this OAuth as in “Slack user installs app and bot inherits user perms” or is it pure service-to-service with a bot identity?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: pure service-to-service for the data plane calls. Slack interaction is user-triggered, but the backend calls are from bot service identity. we’ll include `acting_user` in the audit payload from Slack request context.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side, that’s what we want: bot always works, but we can audit who clicked “Show diff” etc.

Ethan Park: ok so split:
- bot has baseline scopes (deploy context + safe telemetry summaries)
- “expand” actions might require elevated scopes or break-glass?

Marcus Lin: careful: “expand” tends to be where leakage happens. i’d rather keep bot scopes limited and gate any more detailed views behind explicit, audited “break-glass” or per-rotation role.

Miles Thornton: bot UX already has two tiers in the mock:
- card summary (safe)
- button: “View full diff” / “Open telemetry breakdown”
We can make the second path do an extra auth check.

Benji Okafor: config diff output is always redacted (allowlist + secret patterns). but there’s still “sensitive-ish” (customer routing policy, quotas). so yeah still treat as privileged.

Kevin Osei: audit events should capture *what type* of privileged operation happened. ex:
- CONFIG_DIFF_READ
- PRIV_TELEMETRY_QUERY
- BREAK_GLASS_GRANT / BREAK_GLASS_USE
Also include `tenant_id`/`org_id` when applicable, and `incident_id` if present.

Jordan Lee: we can standardize an event schema:
fields:
- event_name
- actor_type (service)
- actor_id (incident-bot)
- acting_user (slack user id/email if we have it)
- reason (e.g., "incident_triage")
- incident_id (from thread/incident command context)
- resource_type (config/metrics)
- resource_id (tenant_id + service + region)
- decision (allow/deny)
- policy_version

Kaitlyn Nguyen: sounds right. for scopes, proposing:
Baseline bot scopes:
- `deploy.metadata.read` (recent deploys card)
- `telemetry.saturation.read` (gpu util/queue depth/kv cache pressure *aggregated only*)
- `slo.burn.read` (error budget burn link gen)
Privileged scopes (only when user clicks expand AND they’re in allowed group / break-glass):
- `config.diff.read`
- `telemetry.tenant_query.read` (tenant-aware query primitives)

Ethan Park: does `telemetry.saturation.read` hit the same query service as tenant-aware queries? want to avoid “baseline scope still lets you do arbitrary queries”

Logan Wright (via Miles, pasted): baseline saturation can be a separate endpoint with fixed query templates + hard-coded label filters. tenant_query is the flexible one. keep them separate.

Devon Price: implementation detail: Slack action -> bot backend -> call authz service “can user X request privileged operation Y for incident Z?”
If allowed, bot uses *same* service token but with a different scope? or do we mint a short-lived token with elevated scope?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: mint short-lived token with elevated scope via internal auth service. scope bound + TTL 5m. include `acting_user` + `incident_id` claims.

Marcus Lin: +1 short-lived. also require that the privileged token includes `purpose=incident` and `channel=#incidents|#eng-oncall` (or at least source context). if someone replays it elsewhere, it should fail.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can enforce claim `purpose=incident_triage` and `incident_id` present for `config.diff.read` and `telemetry.tenant_query.read`.

Benji Okafor: for break-glass: do we pre-provision “OncallIncidentBotPrivileged” group membership, or do we have an explicit “request break-glass” button?

Rafael Mendes: for the beta, simplest is group-based: SRE oncall rotation + platform oncall have it. Longer term, we can do “request break-glass” with approval trail.

Kevin Osei: group-based still needs audit. every privileged read emits event; monthly review pulls from SIEM.

Ethan Park: and what about bot *itself* calling privileged endpoints in degraded mode? e.g., if auth service is down.

Miles Thornton: if authz is down we should fail closed for privileged (show link + “permissions check unavailable”), but still show baseline cards. no “silent allow” during outage.

Marcus Lin: yes. fail closed for privileged.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ok, action items:
- Kaitlyn: write up final scopes list + claims requirements and drop in ADR comment (today)
- Jordan: update policy engine rules for `config.diff.read` + `telemetry.tenant_query.read` with required claims + deny-by-default (tomorrow)
- Benji: ensure config diff endpoint emits audit event with schema above and includes redaction version/hash (this week)
- Kevin: confirm event names + SIEM routing + retention reqs, share list of required fields (today)
- Devon/Miles: bot path mints 5m privileged token on “expand” + passes acting_user/incident_id + handles fail-closed (this week)

Kevin Osei: for event naming, propose:
- `audit.incident_bot.config_diff.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.telemetry.tenant_query.read`
- `audit.incident_bot.break_glass.token_issued`
Keep in lower_snake or dotted, but consistent.

Marcus Lin: + include `redaction_profile` and `result_count` (e.g., diff lines) to help spot weird usage patterns.

Devon Price: ack. for acting_user, we can map Slack user -> email via SCIM. if missing, log slack user id.

Ethan Park: please also log `service` + `region` on telemetry queries. otherwise it’s useless in reviews.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: got it. i’ll send a short summary in-thread once the doc is updated.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: once scopes are final, we’ll update the bot env + start staging validation.

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post policy diff PR link here when ready.

source.content:
Chloe Martin: hey all — quick sync thread on Console UI for Dedicated burst. need to surface burst usage + limit state in a way CS/customers can understand. we’re aiming to ship the first pass behind the dedicated-burst feature flag.

Chloe Martin: questions:
1) what’s the “source of truth” API shape for usage + limits (control plane read)?
2) what do we show for denial reasons (counts vs last error)?
3) any gotchas around freshness/prop delay that we should set expectations on in UI?

Dylan Brooks: +1. also we need something we can screenshot for sales/CS enablement. doesn’t have to be perfect but has to be defensible.

Alicia Perez: dropping the latest Figma frame here (WIP): https://figma.com/file/ri-console-burst/console-dedicated-burst?node-id=1824%3A9312

Alicia Perez: concept is a “Burst” card on the Dedicated capacity page:
- Status: Enabled/Disabled (read-only initially)
- Baseline (reserved GPUs) + Burst max (cap)
- “Currently bursting” indicator when above baseline
- Usage chart (last 24h) with baseline line + cap line
- Denials: donut by reason

Alicia Perez: screenshot ref (from Figma export): https://drive.google.com/file/d/1PZk3f_burstCard_v3/view (shows warning state at 90% cap)

Ethan Park: API: the read path is in the control plane service; Chloe you should be unblocked by the endpoints in PR-23905 ("console-api-endpoints-for-burst-usage").

Ethan Park: for v1 I’d suggest Console reads:
- tenant burst config (enabled, max_burst_ratio OR absolute cap, cooldown_seconds, priority_tier)
- current utilization snapshot (baseline_gpus, current_effective_gpus, burst_gpus)
- usage aggregates (gpu_seconds_burst_{1h,24h}, burst_tokens_{1h,24h})
- denial aggregates (count by reason last 1h/24h)

Benji Okafor: +1. the endpoints are:
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-usage?window=1h|24h
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-denials?window=1h|24h

Benji Okafor: response times are ok in staging, but note: usage is derived from metering pipeline + cached aggregates. expect some lag.

Logan Wright: re freshness: we should not imply “real-time” for usage. the current aggregate job is on 1m buckets but end-to-end can be 2–5m depending on region + ingestion.

Logan Wright: recommend UI copy like “Usage updates every few minutes.” and a small “Last updated: <timestamp>” label so Support can point at it.

Allison Grant: + add the “last_updated_at” in the API. please don’t try to infer from client time.

Chloe Martin: got it. can we include a “data delayed” banner if last_updated_at > 10m? (we’ve had ppl open tickets for stale quota values…)

Logan Wright: yes, that’s reasonable. also we should not break the page if denials endpoint times out — just show “—” and a tooltip.

Sergio Costa: for the denials donut, keep the category list constrained. reason codes we standardized in pr-23795 should map 1:1 in UI:
- TENANT_BURST_CAP_REACHED
- COOLDOWN_ACTIVE
- POOL_SATURATED
- FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN
- POLICY_DENIED (catch-all)

Sergio Costa: avoid rendering raw strings; use human labels + “Learn more” link to docs.

Alicia Perez: yep. I have friendly labels:
- “Burst cap reached”
- “Cooldown window”
- “Burst pool saturated”
- “Fleet safety limit”
- “Policy denied”

Alicia Perez: plus a tooltip: “Denials are expected when guardrails activate. Your baseline capacity is not impacted.”

Dylan Brooks: baseline not impacted messaging is important. customers are going to assume denied = outage.

Chloe Martin: on “limit state”: do we show cap as ratio vs absolute GPUs? product wants max burst ratio default but contracts might use absolute.

Ethan Park: config supports both but we normalize to an effective absolute cap per region/hw class when we render. for UI: show BOTH if we can:
- “Burst limit: up to 2.0× baseline (max 24 GPUs)”

Ethan Park: if baseline changes (contract amendment) the ratio remains meaningful; the absolute max avoids confusion.

Rachel Kim: from CS perspective, please show what the system will actually enforce right now (the absolute cap), and keep ratio as secondary text.

Alicia Perez: ack. design tweak: main line = “Max burst: 24 GPUs” secondary = “(2.0× baseline)”

Chloe Martin: what about showing “currently bursting”? we’ll compute current_effective_gpus > baseline_gpus.

Benji Okafor: yes, but make sure you’re using the *effective* baseline for the tenant in that region/hw class, not whatever sales wrote in a note :)

Chloe Martin: lol. ok.

Sergio Costa: chart suggestion: stacked area for baseline vs burst usage makes it visually clear. baseline constant, burst is the top layer.

Alicia Perez: that’s what I did in the frame. baseline grey, burst blue overlay. warning state if burst > 80% cap, critical at 95%.

Allison Grant: careful on thresholds — those will generate “why is it red” questions. maybe just show a neutral chart and only show warning when customer is getting denials (or when they hit cap)?

Dylan Brooks: +1. if we warn at 80% but they’re fine, we create noise.

Alicia Perez: ok, compromise: keep a subtle “approaching limit” at 90% but only show the banner when denial_rate > 0 in last 5m OR when burst == cap.

Logan Wright: we can support that if you query a short window (5m) denial counter. but we currently only expose 1h/24h in the endpoint.

Ethan Park: we can add window=5m, but I don’t want to explode API surface last minute. alternative: Console can just use 1h denials for the banner and it’ll still be directionally correct.

Chloe Martin: I’d rather keep v1 simple. 1h denials is fine.

Rachel Kim: +1, CS doesn’t need 5m precision. just need to explain “you’re being throttled by cap/pool/etc.”

Chloe Martin: screenshots — I can grab staging once endpoints are on the console env. Alicia, can you also export a static “happy path” and “cap reached” so enablement has something even if staging is flaky?

Alicia Perez: yes. will export both states + a “data delayed” state.

Alicia Perez: also have a “Learn more” link placeholder. do we have docs URL yet?

Dylan Brooks: docs PR is in-flight (redwood-docs/pr-928). for now link to /docs/dedicated/burst (it’ll 302 until published).

Sergio Costa: for internal-only, also link “View burst dashboards” (Grafana). but make it hidden unless the user has internal role.

Chloe Martin: yep, we can RBAC-gate an “Internal tools” section.

Allison Grant: please don’t embed dashboard iframes. just links.

Benji Okafor: one more gotcha: config changes have propagation delay (policy engine + caches). UI should show “Changes may take up to ~5 minutes to apply.” especially for beta.

Chloe Martin: noted. will add small helper text under the config summary.

Dylan Brooks: action items:
- Chloe: implement Burst card using the 3 read endpoints + last_updated + delayed banner + helper text.
- Alicia: export 3 screenshots (happy, cap reached, delayed) + update to make absolute cap primary.
- Ethan/Benji: confirm staging has PR-23905 deployed by EOD so Chloe can verify.

Ethan Park: staging deploy is queued. I’ll ping when it’s live.

Alicia Perez: I’ll post updated exports here once done.

Chloe Martin: thanks all. will sync with CS once we have the screenshot bundle.

source.messages:
Jordan Lee: Kicking off a thread on rollout policy guardrail edge cases. We keep running into ambiguities in reviews: (1) step size vs approval gates, (2) soak time overrides, (3) admin roles / who can bypass. Want to align before we lock policy engine semantics for GA.

Paula Mendes: +1. This is the stuff customers will hit immediately. For Hosted GA I want the Console to have predictable errors + a path forward (not just “denied”).

Benji Okafor: Starting list of edge cases:
- If max_step_percent=10 and you have approvals required at >=25%, can you jump 10->25 (15pt jump) with approval? or still blocked?
- If min_soak=30m, can SRE/owner force-progress early (like 5m) if metrics are clean?
- Who is “admin”? tenant_admin vs org_owner vs rollout_approver.

Ethan Park: Also: what happens when policy changes mid-rollout. E.g. admin tightens max_step from 25->5 while rollout is paused at 10%.

Jordan Lee: Yup. My current leaning: approvals are additive, not a bypass. I.e. approval gate doesn’t let you exceed max step. Approval just authorizes actions that are otherwise allowed.

Paula Mendes: That’s the safest, but then UX needs to explain why “approved” still can’t go 10->25.

Chloe Martin: From Console POV we can handle it if policy engine returns structured reason: `max_step_exceeded` vs `approval_required`. We’ll show both if needed.

Benji Okafor: Concrete example:
- baseline at 0%
- step schedule: 1, 5, 25, 50, 100
- policy: max_step=10, approvals_required_at=[25,50,100]
If approvals don’t bypass, schedule is invalid unless user inserts intermediate steps (15, 25). That’s OK, but wizard should catch it on “Review”.

Jordan Lee: Agree. We should validate schedule at creation time (and also at execution if step edited).

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator can enforce at “advance step” time only. Creation-time validation is nicer for UX but we still need runtime enforcement for API callers.

Paula Mendes: Make it both. Wizard preview should flag “this schedule can’t run under current policy”. API should return same code.

Sean Gallagher: On soak time: strong preference that min soak is not bypassable by tenant users. If we let people skip soak, they’ll do it under pressure and then blame us when it goes sideways.

Rafael Mendes: +1. For reliability we want soak time to mean something. If we allow overrides, it should be a separate “break glass” role that’s very explicit + audited.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: On roles: today we have tenant_admin and a few internal roles. The new RBAC PR introduces `rollout_approver`. We should be careful not to implicitly treat tenant_admin as “can do everything” because enterprise customers expect separation of duties.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: bypasses should be extremely limited. If we introduce any “override” mechanism, it needs:
1) explicit permission (separate from tenant_admin)
2) audit event with reason string
3) customer-visible activity log entry

Jordan Lee: So a possible model:
- max_step_percent: hard limit, never bypassed
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; internal SRE can bypass only with break-glass
- approval gates: require `rollout_approver` to proceed past configured thresholds; does not relax other constraints

Paula Mendes: I’m good with that for GA, but can we define what “thresholds” mean? Is it “when canary_percent >= X” or “when you increase by >= X”? Customers asked “approval at 50%” meaning “before you go to 50% or higher”.

Benji Okafor: I assumed “target percent”. So going to 50 triggers approval. Going 49->51 triggers too.

Jordan Lee: Same. Gate = approval required for any action that results in `target_canary_percent >= gate_percent`.

Ethan Park: Edge: rollback action. If you roll back from 50 to 0, do you need approval? (some orgs require approval for any traffic changes, including rollback)

Paula Mendes: For GA I think rollback should be always allowed to anyone who can manage rollouts. That’s safety.

Marcus Lin: Usually “emergency changes” are permitted. But we can still require audit (who rolled back). I’d avoid requiring approval to roll back.

Sean Gallagher: Please don’t gate rollback. We need fast mitigation.

Jordan Lee: Ok: rollback exempt from approval gates. Still subject to permission (operator role) and audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Then we need two perms:
- `rollouts.execute` (create/start/pause/advance/rollback)
- `rollouts.approve` (approve gated steps)
Tenant_admin can be configured to have both but not implied.

Chloe Martin: Wizard copy: when approval is required, we can show “Pending approval by Rollout Approver” + list eligible roles. Also show link to docs.

Benji Okafor: Back to policy changes mid-rollout:
If max_step lowers, we don’t want already-executed steps to become “invalid”. But next action should evaluate against current policy.

Jordan Lee: Yup. Policy evaluated at action time. Rollout object stores what happened; policy just decides if the *next* action is allowed.

Ethan Park: That implies a weird case: rollout created with 25% step, then admin tightens max_step to 10 before hitting it. Now it’s blocked unless user edits schedule.

Paula Mendes: That’s acceptable, but Console should explain “policy changed since creation” and offer “Edit schedule” CTA.

Hannah Rivera: We’ll need a dedicated error code for that. Otherwise it looks like a random failure.

Jordan Lee: Proposed error taxonomy (for Console + API):
- `ROLL_POLICY_MAX_STEP_EXCEEDED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_MIN_SOAK_NOT_MET`
- `ROLL_POLICY_APPROVAL_REQUIRED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_ACTION_NOT_PERMITTED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_POLICY_CHANGED_BLOCKING`

Benji Okafor: Another edge: min soak per step vs total soak. I think it’s per step (time since last traffic change), right?

Sean Gallagher: Per step. We care that each increase gets a soak. Otherwise people do 1% then wait 30m then instantly go 5->100.

Jordan Lee: Per step: `now - last_traffic_shift_ts >= min_soak_duration`.

Rafael Mendes: Also: if auto-pause triggered (SLO breach), do we enforce a new min soak after resume? I’d say yes (since resume may include a step increase).

Jordan Lee: Resume itself shouldn’t require soak; only advancing traffic does.

Paula Mendes: Can we clarify “pause” vs “stop” semantics in policy? E.g. auto-stop might force rollback. That shouldn’t be blocked by “min soak not met”.

Jordan Lee: Good call: system-initiated safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass soak + approval checks but still log as system actor.

Marcus Lin: Make sure audit distinguishes:
- actor_type: user / system / internal
- if internal override, include ticket/incident ref if available

Chloe Martin: We can display as “System (Auto-stop) paused rollout due to p95 latency threshold breach.” That’s consistent with timeline designs.

Benji Okafor: One more: “approval required at 25%” and max_step=10. User at 20% wants to go to 25% (5pt jump). Needs approval, but the approval action itself—do we require approval before setting target=25, or can they set it and it stays pending?

Jordan Lee: I’d prefer two-step:
1) proposer sets next target (pending)
2) approver approves and then action executes
That avoids the weirdness of “approval required” blocking even creating the pending request.

Paula Mendes: +1. Console flow already has “Submit for approval”.

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator will need a “pending_approval” state for step transitions. We already have a draft state machine; I’ll make sure it covers this.

Sean Gallagher: If it’s pending approval, traffic shouldn’t change until approved (obvious, but calling it out).

Jordan Lee: Summarizing decisions (unless objections):
- Approval gates do NOT bypass max step or min soak.
- max_step_percent: hard limit, no bypass.
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; only internal break-glass can bypass (not in GA UI).
- Approval required when resulting target_canary_percent >= gate threshold.
- Rollback is never approval-gated; always allowed for operators.
- Policy evaluated at action time; policy changes can block future steps.
- System safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass approval/soak but are audited as system actor.
- Implement pending approval step transition.

Paula Mendes: Works for me. I’ll add this to PM-1851 notes + call out in docs as “Approvals don’t override safety limits”.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update policy bundle examples + add unit tests for 10->25 case + policy-change-mid-rollout case.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll double-check RBAC mapping so tenant_admin isn’t implicitly granted `rollouts.approve`.

Marcus Lin: Please include a “reason” field in any override audit event even if we don’t surface override in GA. We’ll need it later.

Jordan Lee: Done. I’ll capture as an ADR comment in ENG-9146 and link this thread.
