Marcus Lin: Usually “emergency changes” are permitted. But we can still require audit (who rolled back). I’d avoid requiring approval to roll back.

Sean Gallagher: Please don’t gate rollback. We need fast mitigation.

Jordan Lee: Ok: rollback exempt from approval gates. Still subject to permission (operator role) and audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Then we need two perms:
- `rollouts.execute` (create/start/pause/advance/rollback)
- `rollouts.approve` (approve gated steps)
Tenant_admin can be configured to have both but not implied.

Chloe Martin: Wizard copy: when approval is required, we can show “Pending approval by Rollout Approver” + list eligible roles. Also show link to docs.

Benji Okafor: Back to policy changes mid-rollout:
If max_step lowers, we don’t want already-executed steps to become “invalid”. But next action should evaluate against current policy.

Jordan Lee: Yup. Policy evaluated at action time. Rollout object stores what happened; policy just decides if the *next* action is allowed.

Ethan Park: That implies a weird case: rollout created with 25% step, then admin tightens max_step to 10 before hitting it. Now it’s blocked unless user edits schedule.

Paula Mendes: That’s acceptable, but Console should explain “policy changed since creation” and offer “Edit schedule” CTA.

Hannah Rivera: We’ll need a dedicated error code for that. Otherwise it looks like a random failure.

Jordan Lee: Proposed error taxonomy (for Console + API):
- `ROLL_POLICY_MAX_STEP_EXCEEDED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_MIN_SOAK_NOT_MET`
- `ROLL_POLICY_APPROVAL_REQUIRED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_ACTION_NOT_PERMITTED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_POLICY_CHANGED_BLOCKING`

Benji Okafor: Another edge: min soak per step vs total soak. I think it’s per step (time since last traffic change), right?

Sean Gallagher: Per step. We care that each increase gets a soak. Otherwise people do 1% then wait 30m then instantly go 5->100.

Jordan Lee: Per step: `now - last_traffic_shift_ts >= min_soak_duration`.

Rafael Mendes: Also: if auto-pause triggered (SLO breach), do we enforce a new min soak after resume? I’d say yes (since resume may include a step increase).

Jordan Lee: Resume itself shouldn’t require soak; only advancing traffic does.

Paula Mendes: Can we clarify “pause” vs “stop” semantics in policy? E.g. auto-stop might force rollback. That shouldn’t be blocked by “min soak not met”.

Jordan Lee: Good call: system-initiated safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass soak + approval checks but still log as system actor.

Marcus Lin: Make sure audit distinguishes:
- actor_type: user / system / internal
- if internal override, include ticket/incident ref if available

Chloe Martin: We can display as “System (Auto-stop) paused rollout due to p95 latency threshold breach.” That’s consistent with timeline designs.

Benji Okafor: One more: “approval required at 25%” and max_step=10. User at 20% wants to go to 25% (5pt jump). Needs approval, but the approval action itself—do we require approval before setting target=25, or can they set it and it stays pending?

Jordan Lee: I’d prefer two-step:
1) proposer sets next target (pending)
2) approver approves and then action executes
That avoids the weirdness of “approval required” blocking even creating the pending request.

Paula Mendes: +1. Console flow already has “Submit for approval”.

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator will need a “pending_approval” state for step transitions. We already have a draft state machine; I’ll make sure it covers this.

Sean Gallagher: If it’s pending approval, traffic shouldn’t change until approved (obvious, but calling it out).

Jordan Lee: Summarizing decisions (unless objections):
- Approval gates do NOT bypass max step or min soak.
- max_step_percent: hard limit, no bypass.
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; only internal break-glass can bypass (not in GA UI).
- Approval required when resulting target_canary_percent >= gate threshold.
- Rollback is never approval-gated; always allowed for operators.
- Policy evaluated at action time; policy changes can block future steps.
- System safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass approval/soak but are audited as system actor.
- Implement pending approval step transition.

Paula Mendes: Works for me. I’ll add this to PM-1851 notes + call out in docs as “Approvals don’t override safety limits”.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update policy bundle examples + add unit tests for 10->25 case + policy-change-mid-rollout case.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll double-check RBAC mapping so tenant_admin isn’t implicitly granted `rollouts.approve`.

Marcus Lin: Please include a “reason” field in any override audit event even if we don’t surface override in GA. We’ll need it later.

Jordan Lee: Done. I’ll capture as an ADR comment in ENG-9146 and link this thread.
text:
Jordan Lee: Kicking off a thread on rollout policy guardrail edge cases. We keep running into ambiguities in reviews: (1) step size vs approval gates, (2) soak time overrides, (3) admin roles / who can bypass. Want to align before we lock policy engine semantics for GA.

Paula Mendes: +1. This is the stuff customers will hit immediately. For Hosted GA I want the Console to have predictable errors + a path forward (not just “denied”).

Benji Okafor: Starting list of edge cases:
- If max_step_percent=10 and you have approvals required at >=25%, can you jump 10->25 (15pt jump) with approval? or still blocked?
- If min_soak=30m, can SRE/owner force-progress early (like 5m) if metrics are clean?
- Who is “admin”? tenant_admin vs org_owner vs rollout_approver.

Ethan Park: Also: what happens when policy changes mid-rollout. E.g. admin tightens max_step from 25->5 while rollout is paused at 10%.

Jordan Lee: Yup. My current leaning: approvals are additive, not a bypass. I.e. approval gate doesn’t let you exceed max step. Approval just authorizes actions that are otherwise allowed.

Paula Mendes: That’s the safest, but then UX needs to explain why “approved” still can’t go 10->25.

Chloe Martin: From Console POV we can handle it if policy engine returns structured reason: `max_step_exceeded` vs `approval_required`. We’ll show both if needed.

Benji Okafor: Concrete example:
- baseline at 0%
- step schedule: 1, 5, 25, 50, 100
- policy: max_step=10, approvals_required_at=[25,50,100]
If approvals don’t bypass, schedule is invalid unless user inserts intermediate steps (15, 25). That’s OK, but wizard should catch it on “Review”.

Jordan Lee: Agree. We should validate schedule at creation time (and also at execution if step edited).

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator can enforce at “advance step” time only. Creation-time validation is nicer for UX but we still need runtime enforcement for API callers.

Paula Mendes: Make it both. Wizard preview should flag “this schedule can’t run under current policy”. API should return same code.

Sean Gallagher: On soak time: strong preference that min soak is not bypassable by tenant users. If we let people skip soak, they’ll do it under pressure and then blame us when it goes sideways.

Rafael Mendes: +1. For reliability we want soak time to mean something. If we allow overrides, it should be a separate “break glass” role that’s very explicit + audited.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: On roles: today we have tenant_admin and a few internal roles. The new RBAC PR introduces `rollout_approver`. We should be careful not to implicitly treat tenant_admin as “can do everything” because enterprise customers expect separation of duties.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: bypasses should be extremely limited. If we introduce any “override” mechanism, it needs:
1) explicit permission (separate from tenant_admin)
2) audit event with reason string
3) customer-visible activity log entry

Jordan Lee: So a possible model:
- max_step_percent: hard limit, never bypassed
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; internal SRE can bypass only with break-glass
- approval gates: require `rollout_approver` to proceed past configured thresholds; does not relax other constraints

Paula Mendes: I’m good with that for GA, but can we define what “thresholds” mean? Is it “when canary_percent >= X” or “when you increase by >= X”? Customers asked “approval at 50%” meaning “before you go to 50% or higher”.

Benji Okafor: I assumed “target percent”. So going to 50 triggers approval. Going 49->51 triggers too.

Jordan Lee: Same. Gate = approval required for any action that results in `target_canary_percent >= gate_percent`.

Ethan Park: Edge: rollback action. If you roll back from 50 to 0, do you need approval? (some orgs require approval for any traffic changes, including rollback)

Paula Mendes: For GA I think rollback should be always allowed to anyone who can manage rollouts. That’s safety.

Marcus Lin: Usually “emergency changes” are permitted. But we can still require audit (who rolled back). I’d avoid requiring approval to roll back.

Sean Gallagher: Please don’t gate rollback. We need fast mitigation.

Jordan Lee: Ok: rollback exempt from approval gates. Still subject to permission (operator role) and audit.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Then we need two perms:
- `rollouts.execute` (create/start/pause/advance/rollback)
- `rollouts.approve` (approve gated steps)
Tenant_admin can be configured to have both but not implied.

Chloe Martin: Wizard copy: when approval is required, we can show “Pending approval by Rollout Approver” + list eligible roles. Also show link to docs.

Benji Okafor: Back to policy changes mid-rollout:
If max_step lowers, we don’t want already-executed steps to become “invalid”. But next action should evaluate against current policy.

Jordan Lee: Yup. Policy evaluated at action time. Rollout object stores what happened; policy just decides if the *next* action is allowed.

Ethan Park: That implies a weird case: rollout created with 25% step, then admin tightens max_step to 10 before hitting it. Now it’s blocked unless user edits schedule.

Paula Mendes: That’s acceptable, but Console should explain “policy changed since creation” and offer “Edit schedule” CTA.

Hannah Rivera: We’ll need a dedicated error code for that. Otherwise it looks like a random failure.

Jordan Lee: Proposed error taxonomy (for Console + API):
- `ROLL_POLICY_MAX_STEP_EXCEEDED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_MIN_SOAK_NOT_MET`
- `ROLL_POLICY_APPROVAL_REQUIRED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_ACTION_NOT_PERMITTED`
- `ROLL_POLICY_POLICY_CHANGED_BLOCKING`

Benji Okafor: Another edge: min soak per step vs total soak. I think it’s per step (time since last traffic change), right?

Sean Gallagher: Per step. We care that each increase gets a soak. Otherwise people do 1% then wait 30m then instantly go 5->100.

Jordan Lee: Per step: `now - last_traffic_shift_ts >= min_soak_duration`.

Rafael Mendes: Also: if auto-pause triggered (SLO breach), do we enforce a new min soak after resume? I’d say yes (since resume may include a step increase).

Jordan Lee: Resume itself shouldn’t require soak; only advancing traffic does.

Paula Mendes: Can we clarify “pause” vs “stop” semantics in policy? E.g. auto-stop might force rollback. That shouldn’t be blocked by “min soak not met”.

Jordan Lee: Good call: system-initiated safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass soak + approval checks but still log as system actor.

Marcus Lin: Make sure audit distinguishes:
- actor_type: user / system / internal
- if internal override, include ticket/incident ref if available

Chloe Martin: We can display as “System (Auto-stop) paused rollout due to p95 latency threshold breach.” That’s consistent with timeline designs.

Benji Okafor: One more: “approval required at 25%” and max_step=10. User at 20% wants to go to 25% (5pt jump). Needs approval, but the approval action itself—do we require approval before setting target=25, or can they set it and it stays pending?

That avoids the weirdness of “approval required” blocking even creating the pending request.

Paula Mendes: +1. Console flow already has “Submit for approval”.

Hannah Rivera: Orchestrator will need a “pending_approval” state for step transitions. We already have a draft state machine; I’ll make sure it covers this.

Sean Gallagher: If it’s pending approval, traffic shouldn’t change until approved (obvious, but calling it out).

Jordan Lee: Summarizing decisions (unless objections):
- Approval gates do NOT bypass max step or min soak.
- max_step_percent: hard limit, no bypass.
- min_soak_duration: hard limit for customers; only internal break-glass can bypass (not in GA UI).
- Approval required when resulting target_canary_percent >= gate threshold.
- Rollback is never approval-gated; always allowed for operators.
- Policy evaluated at action time; policy changes can block future steps.
- System safety actions (auto-pause/auto-rollback) bypass approval/soak but are audited as system actor.
- Implement pending approval step transition.

Paula Mendes: Works for me. I’ll add this to PM-1851 notes + call out in docs as “Approvals don’t override safety limits”.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update policy bundle examples + add unit tests for 10->25 case + policy-change-mid-rollout case.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll double-check RBAC mapping so tenant_admin isn’t implicitly granted `rollouts.approve`.

Marcus Lin: Please include a “reason” field in any override audit event even if we don’t surface override in GA. We’ll need it later.

source.messages:
Cole Summers: Heads up: while wiring burst metering -> billing pipeline, I’m seeing a few edge cases around retries/idempotency that we should nail down before we tell Finance “numbers are clean”.

Cole Summers: Current plan (per PRD) is we emit `BurstUsageEvent` per request w/ fields: tenant_id, region, hw_class, model, burst_gpu_ms, tokens_out, tokens_in, admitted=true/false, denial_reason. The retry problem: gateway retries on 5xx/timeout and we can double-count if we emit on both attempts.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Billing pipeline today assumes events are append-only and unique by event_id. If we can’t guarantee uniqueness, reconciliation gets ugly fast.

Ethan Park: Can we rely on existing request ids from gateway? We have `x-redwood-request-id` (uuid) + optional `x-idempotency-key` (customer supplied) in Hosted. Dedicated gateway also sets a stable request id per attempt but I don’t think it’s stable across retries yet.

Benji Okafor: For Dedicated, we have envoy generating `x-request-id` per hop; it changes on retry unless we pin it. We can add a separate header that survives retry (generated once at edge).

Jordan Lee: Also need to be clear what “retry” means for burst. Is the unit we bill for “admitted capacity” or “actually consumed GPU time”? If we bill on admit but the request never executes (timeout before dispatch), that’s a mismatch.

Cole Summers: Exactly. I think we should only meter when the request actually starts execution on a burst GPU (or when the scheduler assigns burst slot). Not on admit.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we can emit a “start” and “end” event (or a single end event w/ duration). But we have to keep label cardinality under control. Event id should be a field in payload, not a metric label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t shove request_id into Prometheus labels :slightly_smiling_face: We can do logs/OTel events for per-request audit and keep Prom metrics aggregate.

Cole Summers: Proposal:
1) Introduce `burst_metering_key` stable across gateway retries.
2) Billing event_id = hash(tenant_id + burst_metering_key + burst_phase)
3) Emit usage only from data plane (scheduler/runtime) once we have actual GPU-ms.

Cole Summers: For #2, “burst_phase” might just be “usage_v1” so we don’t accidentally treat later schema as dup.

Nadia Rahman: If we can get a stable key, billing pipeline can do idempotent upsert / dedupe (we currently do exactly-once-ish w/ Kafka + consumer offsets, but retries upstream break assumptions). If you give me `event_id`, we can enforce uniqueness at sink.

Rishi Malhotra: Jumping in — from Finance side we care about two things:
- Customer invoice line items match the contract definition (e.g., burst GPU-seconds or burst token surcharge).
- Daily revenue reporting matches invoiced totals (no phantom usage).
If we tell customers “burst usage is measured by GPU-seconds actually consumed” then you must not charge on admit.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also we need explainability when a customer disputes: “here are the requests/intervals that contributed.” So having per-request audit trail is good, but invoicing is aggregate.

Ethan Park: On stable key: we can add `x-redwood-billing-id` generated at edge (once) and propagated through retries. Similar to idempotency, but platform-owned.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: envoy retry filter doesn’t preserve generated headers by default if upstream resets? We should set it explicitly in gateway code before it hits envoy retries.

Jordan Lee: Another edge case: if admission controller returns “queued” vs “denied”, request might sit, then client times out and retries. We might end up executing both unless we cancel old one. That’s not just billing; that’s correctness.

Cole Summers: For v1 we’re doing “immediate admit/deny” (no queue) per ADR draft, right? So less risk. But we still have internal retries (data plane transient errors) that can re-run dispatch.

Ethan Park: Correct: immediate admit/deny for GA, no customer-visible queue semantics. Internal backpressure still exists but request either goes through baseline or burst or gets 429.

Nadia Rahman: Then we can scope idempotency to: same customer request retried at gateway layer, or data plane “attempts” for the same request.

Logan Wright: Data plane can generate an “execution_id” per attempt. If we have `billing_id` stable, we can guarantee only one execution contributes to billing by taking the first successful execution.

Cole Summers: Yup. Something like:
- stable `billing_id` at edge
- `attempt` increments per internal retry
- emit usage event only for `terminal_state=success|partial_success` with GPU-ms measured
- if multiple success events show up (bug), billing sink dedupes by (tenant_id, billing_id)

Allison Grant: Define what “partial_success” means for streaming. If we stream tokens and then disconnect, GPU time was still consumed. Do we bill that?

Rishi Malhotra: We generally bill for work performed. If we generated 2k tokens and customer disconnected, that’s still cost. So yes, bill what’s actually generated.

Cole Summers: That means metering should be based on server-side token counts + GPU-ms, not client receipt.

Laura Bennett: Contract language needs to be precise: “usage based on Redwood-measured compute/time and tokens generated” etc. Michael/Sofia are drafting but we should sync them on these semantics.

Jordan Lee: Another edge: denied burst but served on baseline. That should not create burst usage. But we might still emit a “denial” event for observability.

Nadia Rahman: Denial events should NOT go into billing sink unless we explicitly separate streams. Otherwise someone will accidentally sum admitted=false events.

Cole Summers: Good call. We can split:
- `BurstUsageEvent` (billable) emitted only when burst pool resources consumed
- `BurstDecisionEvent` (non-billable) for analytics/ops

Logan Wright: Please also keep schema versioned. If you change field names, downstream breaks. Add `schema_version` string.

Ethan Park: What about reconciliation with Finance reporting? Rishi asked daily revenue totals.

Nadia Rahman: We can provide a daily rollup job per tenant:
- sum(burst_gpu_ms) by tenant/day/region/hw_class
- compare to invoice line items produced by billing service
- alert if delta > threshold (e.g. 0.5% or $50)
But again, this only works if upstream events are stable/idempotent.

Cole Summers: Can we get a “replay window” expectation? If we detect a bug, can we re-export from raw logs?

Nadia Rahman: Yes, but it’s painful. If we store raw usage events in the data lake (S3) with partition by day, we can reprocess. I’d rather not rely on that for GA correctness.

Benji Okafor: Action items I see:
1) Add `x-redwood-billing-id` generation at edge + propagate through routing/data plane.
2) Update metering emitter to use billing-id for event_id.
3) Split decision vs usage events.

Allison Grant: + add a short doc in the runbook: “customer disputes burst charges” -> where to pull per-request breakdown (console export or internal debug endpoint).

Cole Summers: I’ll write up a quick design note + update ENG-4827 w/ these semantics. Also going to add a unit test: simulate retry path (same billing-id, two attempts) => exactly 1 billing record.

Rishi Malhotra: Please tag me once you have the definition in writing. Finance sign-off depends on “metering unit” + reconciliation story.

BillingPipelineBot: Reminder: billing-data-prod nightly validation runs at 02:00 UTC. Current dedupe key: `event_id`. If `event_id` is empty or not unique, duplicates will be counted.

Laura Bennett: Thanks all. Once we have the stable billing-id approach, I’m comfortable telling Legal “we can defend the measurement.”

Cole Summers: Cool. I’ll loop in Michael/Sofia separately. Closing loop here after I push the header + dedupe changes.

The retry problem: gateway retries on 5xx/timeout and we can double-count if we emit on both attempts.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Billing pipeline today assumes events are append-only and unique by event_id. If we can’t guarantee uniqueness, reconciliation gets ugly fast.

Ethan Park: Can we rely on existing request ids from gateway? We have `x-redwood-request-id` (uuid) + optional `x-idempotency-key` (customer supplied) in Hosted. Dedicated gateway also sets a stable request id per attempt but I don’t think it’s stable across retries yet.

Benji Okafor: For Dedicated, we have envoy generating `x-request-id` per hop; it changes on retry unless we pin it. We can add a separate header that survives retry (generated once at edge).

Jordan Lee: Also need to be clear what “retry” means for burst. Is the unit we bill for “admitted capacity” or “actually consumed GPU time”? If we bill on admit but the request never executes (timeout before dispatch), that’s a mismatch.

Cole Summers: Exactly. I think we should only meter when the request actually starts execution on a burst GPU (or when the scheduler assigns burst slot). Not on admit.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we can emit a “start” and “end” event (or a single end event w/ duration). But we have to keep label cardinality under control. Event id should be a field in payload, not a metric label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t shove request_id into Prometheus labels :slightly_smiling_face: We can do logs/OTel events for per-request audit and keep Prom metrics aggregate.

Cole Summers: Proposal:
1) Introduce `burst_metering_key` stable across gateway retries.
2) Billing event_id = hash(tenant_id + burst_metering_key + burst_phase)
3) Emit usage only from data plane (scheduler/runtime) once we have actual GPU-ms.

Cole Summers: For #2, “burst_phase” might just be “usage_v1” so we don’t accidentally treat later schema as dup.

Nadia Rahman: If we can get a stable key, billing pipeline can do idempotent upsert / dedupe (we currently do exactly-once-ish w/ Kafka + consumer offsets, but retries upstream break assumptions). If you give me `event_id`, we can enforce uniqueness at sink.

Rishi Malhotra: Jumping in — from Finance side we care about two things:
- Customer invoice line items match the contract definition (e.g., burst GPU-seconds or burst token surcharge).
- Daily revenue reporting matches invoiced totals (no phantom usage).
If we tell customers “burst usage is measured by GPU-seconds actually consumed” then you must not charge on admit.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also we need explainability when a customer disputes: “here are the requests/intervals that contributed.” So having per-request audit trail is good, but invoicing is aggregate.

Ethan Park: On stable key: we can add `x-redwood-billing-id` generated at edge (once) and propagated through retries. Similar to idempotency, but platform-owned.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: envoy retry filter doesn’t preserve generated headers by default if upstream resets? We should set it explicitly in gateway code before it hits envoy retries.

Jordan Lee: Another edge case: if admission controller returns “queued” vs “denied”, request might sit, then client times out and retries. We might end up executing both unless we cancel old one. That’s not just billing; that’s correctness.

Cole Summers: For v1 we’re doing “immediate admit/deny” (no queue) per ADR draft, right? So less risk. But we still have internal retries (data plane transient errors) that can re-run dispatch.

Ethan Park: Correct: immediate admit/deny for GA, no customer-visible queue semantics. Internal backpressure still exists but request either goes through baseline or burst or gets 429.

Nadia Rahman: Then we can scope idempotency to: same customer request retried at gateway layer, or data plane “attempts” for the same request.

Logan Wright: Data plane can generate an “execution_id” per attempt. If we have `billing_id` stable, we can guarantee only one execution contributes to billing by taking the first successful execution.

Cole Summers: Yup. Something like:
- stable `billing_id` at edge
- `attempt` increments per internal retry
- emit usage event only for `terminal_state=success|partial_success` with GPU-ms measured
- if multiple success events show up (bug), billing sink dedupes by (tenant_id, billing_id)

Allison Grant: Define what “partial_success” means for streaming. If we stream tokens and then disconnect, GPU time was still consumed. Do we bill that?

Rishi Malhotra: We generally bill for work performed. If we generated 2k tokens and customer disconnected, that’s still cost. So yes, bill what’s actually generated.

Cole Summers: That means metering should be based on server-side token counts + GPU-ms, not client receipt.

Laura Bennett: Contract language needs to be precise: “usage based on Redwood-measured compute/time and tokens generated” etc. Michael/Sofia are drafting but we should sync them on these semantics.

Jordan Lee: Another edge: denied burst but served on baseline. That should not create burst usage. But we might still emit a “denial” event for observability.

Nadia Rahman: Denial events should NOT go into billing sink unless we explicitly separate streams. Otherwise someone will accidentally sum admitted=false events.

Cole Summers: Good call. We can split:
- `BurstUsageEvent` (billable) emitted only when burst pool resources consumed
- `BurstDecisionEvent` (non-billable) for analytics/ops

Logan Wright: Please also keep schema versioned. If you change field names, downstream breaks. Add `schema_version` string.

Ethan Park: What about reconciliation with Finance reporting? Rishi asked daily revenue totals.

Nadia Rahman: We can provide a daily rollup job per tenant:
- sum(burst_gpu_ms) by tenant/day/region/hw_class
- compare to invoice line items produced by billing service
- alert if delta > threshold (e.g. 0.5% or $50)
But again, this only works if upstream events are stable/idempotent.

Cole Summers: Can we get a “replay window” expectation? If we detect a bug, can we re-export from raw logs?

Nadia Rahman: Yes, but it’s painful. If we store raw usage events in the data lake (S3) with partition by day, we can reprocess. I’d rather not rely on that for GA correctness.

Benji Okafor: Action items I see:
1) Add `x-redwood-billing-id` generation at edge + propagate through routing/data plane.
2) Update metering emitter to use billing-id for event_id.
3) Split decision vs usage events.

Allison Grant: + add a short doc in the runbook: “customer disputes burst charges” -> where to pull per-request breakdown (console export or internal debug endpoint).

Cole Summers: I’ll write up a quick design note + update ENG-4827 w/ these semantics. Also going to add a unit test: simulate retry path (same billing-id, two attempts) => exactly 1 billing record.

Rishi Malhotra: Please tag me once you have the definition in writing. Finance sign-off depends on “metering unit” + reconciliation story.

BillingPipelineBot: Reminder: billing-data-prod nightly validation runs at 02:00 UTC. Current dedupe key: `event_id`. If `event_id` is empty or not unique, duplicates will be counted.

Laura Bennett: Thanks all. Once we have the stable billing-id approach, I’m comfortable telling Legal “we can defend the measurement.”

Cole Summers: Cool. I’ll loop in Michael/Sofia separately. Closing loop here after I push the header + dedupe changes.

source.messages:
Ethan Park: looping in @Naomi Feldman + @Marcus Lin on RBAC/audit for the route-level quantization toggle. We need to lock down who can enable profiles + what we log for diffs.

Benji Okafor: +1. Control plane endpoint is basically `PATCH /v1/routes/{id}` w/ `quantization_profile_ref` + `quantization_enabled`. Today route updates require `route:write` which maps to Org Admin + Project Admin. Question: is that sufficient for beta?

Naomi Feldman: for beta, we can keep it simple but we need to be explicit. For enterprise security reviews, they’ll ask:
- who can change it (roles)
- is there an approval workflow
- can they export an audit trail w/ before/after

Marcus Lin: also needs to cover kill-switch actions. If SRE flips global disable, that should produce an audit event too.

Chloe Martin: Console side: design has “Enable quantization profile” toggle on Route Settings. We can hide it unless user has `route:write`. But some customers want separate permissions for “performance tuning” vs “route config”.

Priya Natarajan: we’re not building a new approval system in v1. But we can support “restricted to admins only” + strong audit logs. Approval workflow can be a future enterprise add-on.

Jada Williams: on audit diffs: are we logging the entire route config JSON? or just the fields touched? If we dump full config, it’s noisy and might include unrelated secrets (even if we don’t store secrets there, customers will worry).

Benji Okafor: proposal: emit audit event for *quantization settings changes* only:
- actor_user_id + actor_email
- org_id / project_id
- route_id + route_name
- old_quantization_profile_ref / new_quantization_profile_ref
- old_enabled / new_enabled
- request_id
- source = console|api|terraform

Naomi Feldman: that’s closer to what auditors expect. We should also capture:
- reason string (optional) if supplied by Console modal
- client_ip / user_agent (if available)
- “effective outcome”: applied vs would-fallback (compatibility)

Ethan Park: careful on “applied vs fallback” — at write time we can validate compatibility for current target deployment tier/region, but actual runtime resolution depends on hardware + routing. We can log “validated=true/false” + warnings.

Marcus Lin: +1. Don’t imply it’s applied everywhere if it’ll fallback on some GPU SKUs.

Chloe Martin: Console can show warnings on save (“will fallback on A10 in us-west”, etc). But audit event should include `compatibility_check: passed|warn|blocked` and the list of warnings as a string.

Jada Williams: for diffs format, can we do an RFC6902-ish patch string? e.g. `replace /quantization_profile_ref default -> int8_v3` so we can render it in change history.

Benji Okafor: easiest for now is old/new fields. Change history UI can render “quantization_profile_ref changed from X to Y; enabled changed from false to true”. JSON patch later.

Kevin Osei: please include stable field names for export → SIEM. If we’re adding new audit event types, we should coordinate with the exporter. Compliance folks will ask for `event_type` + `object_type` + `object_id` consistently.

Naomi Feldman: what do we call the event? Something like:
- `route.quantization_profile.updated`
- `platform.quantization_profiles.killswitch.updated`
Also include `actor_type` (user/service) because some changes may be automated.

AuditBot: reminder: audit event taxonomy doc recommends `object_type`, `action`, `result` fields. (ref: internal /audit/taxonomy)

Priya Natarajan: RBAC question: do we need a distinct permission flag like `route:quantization:write`?

Ethan Park: I’d rather not for v1. Too much surface area. But we can add a guard: only Org Admin / Project Admin can change quant settings even if someone has narrower route write permission (some customers grant route writes to app teams).

Marcus Lin: that’s reasonable. Security posture: treat quantization enablement like “production behavior change” (similar to model version pin). Restrict to elevated roles.

Chloe Martin: ok so: UI visible only for elevated roles. For everyone else show read-only “Quantization: Disabled (admin-only)”?

Naomi Feldman: yes, and doc it. Also need an answer for “break-glass”: in incident, SRE can disable globally. That must be logged and visible.

Jada Williams: change history: we should log both the attempted change and whether it was accepted/blocked. If user tries to enable incompatible profile, that’s still interesting.

Benji Okafor: we already return 4xx w/ structured error when blocked. We can emit an audit event with `result=denied` and include error code.

Kevin Osei: +1, denied attempts matter for compliance.

Ethan Park: summarizing proposed requirements:
1) Write access: only Org Admin + Project Admin (not general route editors) can update quant settings.
2) No approval workflow in v1.
3) Audit events on enable/disable + profile ref changes + denied attempts.
4) Event payload includes old/new fields + actor + route + source + request_id + compatibility_check result + warnings.
5) Separate audit event for kill-switch toggles.

Priya Natarajan: sounds good. Add to PRD/ADR notes? @Naomi Feldman can you sanity check wording for security reviews.

Naomi Feldman: yep. Also add: retention (at least 1 year for enterprise) and export availability. Even if we don’t implement new retention, we need to state current retention.

Chloe Martin: for Console modal, we can add optional “Reason for change” textbox. Stored in audit event `change_reason`.

Marcus Lin: make sure we don’t allow arbitrary PII, but it’s optional. Just store it as-is.

Jada Williams: action items:
- @Benji Okafor update audit event schema + emit on denied attempts
- @Chloe Martin add role gating + reason field in UI
- @Kevin Osei confirm exporter fields + naming
- @Naomi Feldman draft security review answer snippet

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: I’ll open a short tracking task for “RBAC + audit specifics for quant profiles” and link this thread.

source.content:
Logan Wright: kicking off thread on route/model tagging for the new cache metrics. we need slices by route + model (+ version) but we *cannot* blow up series count w/ tenant_id, raw route names, etc. @Nadia Rahman can you sanity check proposed label set + allowlist approach?

Nadia Rahman: yup. reminder from standards: cache metrics should be joinable to gateway context, but labels must be bounded. route label is the biggest footgun.

Ethan Park: current gateway has `route_id` (uuid) + `route_slug` (string) + `tenant_id`. runtime only sees a subset today. i can propagate a stable `route_key` down to runtime, but we need to define what it is.

Amara Diallo: +1. also note: Dedicated and Private customers sometimes have route slugs like "support-agent-prod" etc. we should not put those verbatim on metrics.

Logan Wright: proposal v0:
- required labels: `deployment_mode`, `region`, `model_id`, `model_version_id` (or `model_revision`), `route`
- optional (careful): `customer_tier` (free/pro/ent), `workspace_hash` (??)
- NO `tenant_id`

Nadia Rahman: route label needs to be allowlisted and bounded. best practice: send `route_id` but mapped to a small, controlled enum-ish set.

Thomas Baird: mapping idea: at gateway emit route tags as:
- `route_bucket`: stable string chosen from allowlist (e.g. "chat_default", "embeddings_v2", "rerank", etc)
- `route_id_hash`: only in *traces* not metrics
then metrics slice on route_bucket; traces can drill into the specific route.

Allison Grant: from SRE POV, we need "which route regressed". route_bucket might be too coarse if it collapses everything into 5 buckets.

Caleb Johnson: if we allow raw route_id we’re at risk of 10k+ series per metric per region (route_id * model_version * buckets). ingestion can guard, but then we’ll drop data for the routes you care about.

Ethan Park: could we do a two-tier approach?
1) metrics: `route_group` (bounded) + `route_id` only for a *small allowlist* of top routes
2) everything else gets `route_id="other"`
allowlist maintained by control plane (top N by QPS) and pushed to gateway/runtime.

Paula Mendes: product ask is: breakdown by route for internal ops, and *some* customer value later. okay with initial phase being “top routes + other” as long as it’s explicit in docs.

Allison Grant: top-N allowlist works if N is big enough (and rotates). need to avoid flapping where route falls out of allowlist mid-day.

Caleb Johnson: we can implement guardrails:
- allowlist evaluated daily (or hourly) with hysteresis
- keep routes in list for >= 7 days once admitted
- hard cap per env/region, e.g. 200 routes
and ingestion will *drop* any label combo where route_id not in allowlist unless route_id=="other".

Nadia Rahman: +1. also want consistent naming across runtime + gateway + ingestion. recommend:
- `rw_route_id` (string) but values either allowlisted opaque ids or "other"
- `rw_route_group` (bounded enum-ish)
- `rw_model` (catalog name)
- `rw_model_version` (stable version id, not sha)
- `rw_deployment_mode` (hosted|dedicated|private)
- `rw_region`
No tenant_id. If we need customer segmentation, use `rw_tier` (free|pro|enterprise) which is bounded.

Logan Wright: question: for joinability in analytics, do we need actual route_uuid somewhere?

Ethan Park: gateway logs already have route_uuid + model_version_id + request_id. for metrics, we just need enough to slice. for joining metrics->route metadata we can use `rw_route_id` when allowlisted (since it’ll be the route_uuid). if route not allowlisted it becomes "other".

Thomas Baird: we should document that `rw_route_id` is “high-cardinality controlled label” and is blanked to "other" unless allowlisted.

Allison Grant: also: can we include `rw_model_family`? it’s useful when comparing llama3 vs mistral.

Nadia Rahman: model_family is bounded, fine. but avoid both model_family and model_id if redundant. (or keep both if you promise boundedness)

Logan Wright: ok.
Label set v1:
Required:
- rw_deployment_mode
- rw_region
- rw_model
- rw_model_version
- rw_route_group
Optional/guarded:
- rw_route_id (allowlisted else "other")
Optional/bounded:
- rw_model_family
- rw_tier

Caleb Johnson: from ingestion side, we can enforce:
- if rw_route_id != "other", must match allowlist set for that env
- if label missing / not allowed -> drop or rewrite to other (TBD)
- maximum distinct rw_route_group: 50
- maximum distinct rw_model_version: bounded by model catalog anyway
We should decide rewrite vs drop. rewrite keeps counters consistent but could hide data issues.

Allison Grant: prefer rewrite to `other` for route_id, but *drop* if someone starts sending tenant_id or raw prompt hash.

Nadia Rahman: agree. rewrite only for the controlled label; drop for forbidden labels.

Amara Diallo: impl detail: we need a migration path. right now some services emit `route` as a string slug. we’ll need to keep both for a release or rename.

Ethan Park: i can add new tags at gateway first:
- compute route_group from route config (new field)
- attach route_uuid as route_id
Then runtime metrics pick them up (PR on the runtime side is in flight).
We can keep old `route` label for 1-2 weeks but dashboards should move.

Logan Wright: who owns route_group mapping? control plane or manually in gateway config?

Paula Mendes: product can help define default groups for Hosted (chat/embeddings/rerank) and allow Dedicated to optionally set route_group. for Private, we probably need a generic "customer_defined" group unless they opt in.

Nadia Rahman: careful: if route_group becomes free-form we’re back to cardinality issues. route_group must be allowlisted too (bounded set). For Private maybe route_group is just "private" or "customer" and that’s it.

Caleb Johnson: +1. enforce route_group values against a small set per deployment_mode.

Thomas Baird: operationally, we should put the allowlist in a place both gateway + ingestion can read. simplest: control-plane publishes a JSON blob in config service:
- allowlisted_route_ids (per env/region)
- allowed_route_groups
Gateway uses it to set `rw_route_id` to "other" before emitting metrics. Ingestion re-checks as defense-in-depth.

Allison Grant: defense-in-depth is great. also avoids ingestion costs from junk labels.

Ethan Park: ok i’ll do:
- gateway: if route_uuid not in allowlist => rw_route_id="other"
- always attach rw_route_group (bounded)
- attach rw_model_version from control plane (already have)

Logan Wright: question on model version consistency: is it `model_version_id` or `model_release_id`?

Ethan Park: in gateway we have `model_version_id` (uuid) + `model_alias` (string). runtime currently gets alias. i can pass the version uuid as `rw_model_version`.

Nadia Rahman: use uuid (stable) as rw_model_version, but only if bounded (it is). do not use git sha.

Caleb Johnson: bounded yes, but watch out for “canary versions” per customer. if Dedicated customers upload custom builds, we might get a lot.

Paula Mendes: Dedicated customers can pin versions but they come from our catalog; not per-tenant custom builds in this sprint.

Allison Grant: ok.

Logan Wright: decisions:
1) metrics labels include `rw_route_group` (bounded) and `rw_route_id` (allowlisted else "other").
2) `rw_route_id` uses route_uuid (opaque) ONLY when allowlisted.
3) ingestion enforces allowlist + drops forbidden labels; rewrites non-allowlisted route_id to "other".
4) no tenant_id anywhere.

Action items:
- @Ethan Park @Amara Diallo implement gateway tagging + route_id rewrite to other using config allowlist.
- @Nadia Rahman update schema + standards doc snippet for these labels.
- @Caleb Johnson implement ingestion guard rules + metrics for “dropped labels / rewritten to other”.
- @Thomas Baird propose allowlist refresh policy (cadence + hysteresis) and where blob lives.

Amara Diallo: ack. i’ll start with hosted gateway. dedicated/private gateways might lag by a release, will coordinate.

Caleb Johnson: i’ll add a counter like `telemetry_label_guard_rewrites_total{label="rw_route_id"}` and `telemetry_label_guard_drops_total{reason=...}` so we can detect breakage.

Allison Grant: please also add a dashboard panel: “% of cache metrics under route_id=other” so we can see if allowlist is too small.

Thomas Baird: noted. also, allowlist refresh policy: propose daily job based on 7d trailing QPS, keep top 200 per region, 7-day stickiness.

Nadia Rahman: cool. please make sure docs call out that route_id=other is expected, not a bug.

Logan Wright: perfect. looping this into ENG-4823 comments + linking this thread.

source.messages:
Jordan Lee: quick q on authz decision cache TTLs for the policy-evaluator client. what are we comfortable with for v2? (esp w/ deny-by-default + audits)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: depends what we're caching. if it's “allow/deny for (subject, action, scope)” we need to be really careful w/ role change propagation.

Ines Carvalho: +1. scopes can be org/project/env/cluster. role bindings change at org scope but decision is per-scope. invalidation has to understand hierarchy.

Jordan Lee: current PR draft (client-side cache) is essentially:
- key: subject_id + action + scope_key + policy_bundle_version
- value: decision + reason
- ttl: ???
- negative caching for denies: also ???

Amara Diallo: gateway side: would prefer short TTL for allows, and maybe even shorter for denies? but we also don’t want to hammer evaluator on every request.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side, we can instrument:
- cache_hit / cache_miss
- eval_latency_ms (p50/p95)
- decision_age_ms (now - decision_computed_ts)
- stale_override_count (if we can detect)
But we need a definition of “stale” that’s observable.

Marcus Lin: security POV: worst case is stale ALLOW after privilege removal. stale DENY after granting is annoying but not catastrophic. so TTL should optimize against stale ALLOW.

Ethan Park: control plane: role bindings updates are in cp-db, evaluator reads policy bindings from same store. we can publish “authz bindings changed” events. question: do we want event-driven invalidation, or just TTL-based and accept delay?

Priya Natarajan: goal: deterministic + low latency. event-driven invalidation sounds right but can’t be the only mechanism (events drop). hybrid: TTL + invalidation on updates.

Benji Okafor: what is realistic for propagation? today role/binding writes already go through control plane API. we can hook invalidation there.

Jordan Lee: yep. idea: on role/binding create/update/delete, emit to Redis pubsub / NATS / Kafka (whatever we already have in platform) and clients subscribe to invalidate relevant keys.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: we need to ensure gateway + services can both consume invalidation signals (or shared library). otherwise the gateway cache invalidates but service cache doesn’t.

Chloe Martin: console backend also uses authz checks for admin pages. if we cache there too, same issue.

Ines Carvalho: invalidation granularity: if a user’s bindings change, easiest is “bust everything for subject_id”. that’s safe but may reduce hit rate for “busy” subjects.

Amara Diallo: bust-by-subject might be ok. most traffic is API keys though, not interactive users. do API keys map to subject_id too? (service accounts)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes, key -> principal. but note: key rotation / key disable should invalidate immediately too.

Jordan Lee: ok, so invalidation triggers list so far:
1) role binding changes (add/remove binding)
2) role definition changes (template update / custom role edit)
3) permission catalog/policy bundle version bump
4) principal disable / key revoke
5) org/project/env deletion (?)
Anything else?

Marcus Lin: break-glass activation should bypass caches or force miss (we want audit trail to reflect elevation). maybe break-glass causes a policy version bump or special “session” that’s always evaluated fresh.

Priya Natarajan: for break-glass we already planned time-bound elevation token; just don’t cache across sessions (include elevation_id in cache key) and keep TTL very short.

Logan Wright: re observability: we can’t always know “stale allow” unless we can compare against ground truth. but we can track “decision computed under policy_version=X” and if we later see “policy_version_current=Y” and cache returns X, that’s a stale risk.

Jordan Lee: good point. we can store policy_bundle_version in key; then any bundle bump naturally changes key. but for binding changes w/o bundle bump, we still risk stale.

Ethan Park: bindings changes could increment a monotonic “bindings_etag” per org (or per subject). evaluator can return that etag with decision. cache key includes etag. then on binding change we bump etag.

Ines Carvalho: etag per org is straightforward. per subject is more precise but more writes.

Amara Diallo: org-etag seems workable. but for project/env scoped checks, change in org-level bindings still matters. so org-etag keying is safe.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: but if scope is project, and bindings are project-scoped (future?), then org-etag might be too coarse. though in v2 we still store bindings w/ explicit scope; if we do “etag per scope node” we’re back to hierarchy.

Jordan Lee: for v2 MVP, can we do:
- etag per org
- TTL = 60s for allow, 15s for deny
- immediate invalidation on binding/role changes via best-effort pubsub
…and add more granular etags later?

Marcus Lin: 60s stale-allow window is too long for some enterprise expectations. they’ll ask “how quickly does access revocation take effect?”

Priya Natarajan: agreed. let’s target <10s worst-case. event invalidation should make it near-immediate, but TTL sets worst-case if events fail.

Ethan Park: then TTL for allow should be like 5s? that’s going to reduce cache effectiveness.

Amara Diallo: gateway QPS is high. 5s TTL still gives some wins under bursty traffic (same key hitting repeatedly). plus we can do per-request batching at evaluator? (not sure)

Jordan Lee: alternative: TTL longer, but cache key includes org_etag fetched cheaply. i.e. client keeps org_etag cached separately w/ its own TTL and refreshes opportunistically.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: careful. if org_etag is cached w/ long TTL you reintroduce stale. you’d need org_etag TTL short too, or invalidation for etag updates.

Logan Wright: metrics suggestion: emit counters on “cache_returned_decision_with_etag != current_etag” if we can cheaply check current etag (even sampled). That can quantify stale risk.

Benji Okafor: can we have evaluator return “current_etag” always, and client uses it to update local etag cache? that still doesn’t tell if it’s current unless evaluator computed it.

Ethan Park: simplest: store org_etag in same datastore as bindings. on binding update: bump org_etag. evaluator on eval: reads org_etag + bindings (likely same transaction / same read). client caches decision keyed by org_etag. no need for separate etag fetch.

Jordan Lee: yes. that means cached decision is only valid as long as org_etag unchanged. TTL can then be longer for perf, because staleness is bounded by etag correctness, not time.

Marcus Lin: that helps w/ revocation correctness, assuming org_etag bump is reliable and evaluator reads the latest etag.

Priya Natarajan: if datastore replication lag exists, still possible to read old etag for a bit. but we already have eventual consistency concerns; we should state guarantees: “revocation applies within X seconds” (bounded by replication + cache). make X small.

Amara Diallo: for gateway, we can add a safety valve: if request is “sensitive” (role changes, key rotation, audit export) force cache bypass. we already tag endpoints w/ permissions; we can also tag as `no_cache`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: i like `no_cache` for high-risk operations. also helps w/ debugging “why denied” because we’ll get fresh denial reasons.

Jordan Lee: ok propose:
- decision cache TTL: 30s (allow/deny both), BUT key includes org_etag, so staleness due to binding changes is avoided
- still do best-effort invalidation broadcast to reduce memory growth + speed up eviction
- endpoint annotation can set `cache=disabled` for specific actions

Marcus Lin: fine if org_etag is in the key. need to ensure org_etag increments on:
- role binding CRUD
- role CRUD (custom roles)
- group mapping changes (IdP group -> role bindings)
- principal disable
And make sure private installer bootstrap also bumps.

Ines Carvalho: what about cross-org principals? not a thing. subject belongs to org.

Chloe Martin: console admin page permissions: mostly org-scoped anyway. org_etag fits.

Logan Wright: what metrics do we want in dashboards?
- authz_eval_total{decision, source=cache|evaluator}
- authz_cache_hit_rate
- authz_eval_latency_ms
- authz_etag_mismatch_detected (if we add any sampled recheck)
- authz_denied_reason_code counts
Also track “role_change_to_effective_access_ms” via synthetic test in staging? (write role change, immediately call protected endpoint until allowed/denied flips)

Priya Natarajan: yes. add a canary test that runs every few minutes. that’s the best evidence for “revocation takes effect quickly.”

Ethan Park: implementation detail: where does org_etag live? cp-db `orgs.authz_etag` int64. bump in same transaction as binding write.

Jordan Lee: cool. i’ll update the client + evaluator contract:
- evaluator response includes `org_etag`
- client cache key uses `org_etag`
- cache TTL 30s
- add `X-Redwood-Authz-Cache: hit|miss` header for admin debug? (behind flag)

Amara Diallo: +1 on header. but only for admins / internal, and don’t leak etag values to end users.

Marcus Lin: please don’t expose etag directly. header can be just hit/miss + decision_id.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: on “permission denied” we should include a denial_reason_code that says `BINDING_NOT_FOUND` vs `SCOPE_MISMATCH` etc. helps support.

Jordan Lee: already in flight (reason codes). i’ll make sure cache stores the reason code too.

Logan Wright: if cache stores denial reason, also store `policy_version` / `bundle_version` so support can correlate.

Priya Natarajan: action items:
- Jordan: update PR w/ org_etag cache key + TTL proposal + endpoint opt-out annotation
- Ethan/Benji: confirm org_etag bump coverage on all binding/role/key endpoints
- Logan: draft metrics + dashboard panels; include synthetic propagation SLI
- Marcus/Kaitlyn: review security posture + what we can claim in enterprise questionnaires

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post a design snippet in the PR today.

Amara Diallo: :+1: and please ping me when you have the `cache=disabled` annotation shape; i need to wire it in gateway middleware.

Marcus Lin: also add a note to runbook: if we see elevated denies after a role change, first check etag bump + cache header + policy version.

Logan Wright: i’ll add that to the rollout dashboards doc as well.

Priya Natarajan: thanks all. let’s keep worst-case revocation under 10s in staging tests before we broaden pilots.

Amara Diallo: gateway QPS is high. 5s TTL still gives some wins under bursty traffic (same key hitting repeatedly). plus we can do per-request batching at evaluator? (not sure)

Jordan Lee: alternative: TTL longer, but cache key includes org_etag fetched cheaply. i.e. client keeps org_etag cached separately w/ its own TTL and refreshes opportunistically.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: careful. if org_etag is cached w/ long TTL you reintroduce stale. you’d need org_etag TTL short too, or invalidation for etag updates.

Logan Wright: metrics suggestion: emit counters on “cache_returned_decision_with_etag != current_etag” if we can cheaply check current etag (even sampled). That can quantify stale risk.

Benji Okafor: can we have evaluator return “current_etag” always, and client uses it to update local etag cache? that still doesn’t tell if it’s current unless evaluator computed it.

Ethan Park: simplest: store org_etag in same datastore as bindings. on binding update: bump org_etag. evaluator on eval: reads org_etag + bindings (likely same transaction / same read). client caches decision keyed by org_etag. no need for separate etag fetch.

Jordan Lee: yes. that means cached decision is only valid as long as org_etag unchanged. TTL can then be longer for perf, because staleness is bounded by etag correctness, not time.

Marcus Lin: that helps w/ revocation correctness, assuming org_etag bump is reliable and evaluator reads the latest etag.

Priya Natarajan: if datastore replication lag exists, still possible to read old etag for a bit. but we already have eventual consistency concerns; we should state guarantees: “revocation applies within X seconds” (bounded by replication + cache). make X small.

Amara Diallo: for gateway, we can add a safety valve: if request is “sensitive” (role changes, key rotation, audit export) force cache bypass. we already tag endpoints w/ permissions; we can also tag as `no_cache`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: i like `no_cache` for high-risk operations. also helps w/ debugging “why denied” because we’ll get fresh denial reasons.

Jordan Lee: ok propose:
- decision cache TTL: 30s (allow/deny both), BUT key includes org_etag, so staleness due to binding changes is avoided
- still do best-effort invalidation broadcast to reduce memory growth + speed up eviction
- endpoint annotation can set `cache=disabled` for specific actions

Marcus Lin: fine if org_etag is in the key. need to ensure org_etag increments on:
- role binding CRUD
- role CRUD (custom roles)
- group mapping changes (IdP group -> role bindings)
- principal disable
And make sure private installer bootstrap also bumps.

Ines Carvalho: what about cross-org principals? not a thing. subject belongs to org.

Chloe Martin: console admin page permissions: mostly org-scoped anyway. org_etag fits.

Logan Wright: what metrics do we want in dashboards?
- authz_eval_total{decision, source=cache|evaluator}
- authz_cache_hit_rate
- authz_eval_latency_ms
- authz_etag_mismatch_detected (if we add any sampled recheck)
- authz_denied_reason_code counts
Also track “role_change_to_effective_access_ms” via synthetic test in staging? (write role change, immediately call protected endpoint until allowed/denied flips)

Priya Natarajan: yes. add a canary test that runs every few minutes. that’s the best evidence for “revocation takes effect quickly.”

Ethan Park: implementation detail: where does org_etag live? cp-db `orgs.authz_etag` int64. bump in same transaction as binding write.

Jordan Lee: cool. i’ll update the client + evaluator contract:
- evaluator response includes `org_etag`
- client cache key uses `org_etag`
- cache TTL 30s
- add `X-Redwood-Authz-Cache: hit|miss` header for admin debug? (behind flag)

Amara Diallo: +1 on header. but only for admins / internal, and don’t leak etag values to end users.

Marcus Lin: please don’t expose etag directly. header can be just hit/miss + decision_id.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: on “permission denied” we should include a denial_reason_code that says `BINDING_NOT_FOUND` vs `SCOPE_MISMATCH` etc. helps support.

Jordan Lee: already in flight (reason codes). i’ll make sure cache stores the reason code too.

Logan Wright: if cache stores denial reason, also store `policy_version` / `bundle_version` so support can correlate.

Priya Natarajan: action items:
- Jordan: update PR w/ org_etag cache key + TTL proposal + endpoint opt-out annotation
- Ethan/Benji: confirm org_etag bump coverage on all binding/role/key endpoints
- Logan: draft metrics + dashboard panels; include synthetic propagation SLI
- Marcus/Kaitlyn: review security posture + what we can claim in enterprise questionnaires

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: will post a design snippet in the PR today.

Amara Diallo: :+1: and please ping me when you have the `cache=disabled` annotation shape; i need to wire it in gateway middleware.

Marcus Lin: also add a note to runbook: if we see elevated denies after a role change, first check etag bump + cache header + policy version.

Logan Wright: i’ll add that to the rollout dashboards doc as well.

Priya Natarajan: thanks all. let’s keep worst-case revocation under 10s in staging tests before we broaden pilots.

source.content:
Benji Okafor: heads up, seeing something weird with SIEM export configs post-migration. 2 beta tenants reporting exports “disabled” even though they didn’t touch settings. Anyone else seeing this?

Support Bot: FYI linked internal ticket INT-9041: “Audit export destination flipped to disabled after config API migration”. Tenant: acme-banking-prod. Reporter: CSM.

Ethan Park: ugh. is this after yesterday’s control-plane rollout? do we have a repro / config before+after?

Benji Okafor: rollout was the export config API v2 storage migration (the one that normalizes destinations + filters). We didn’t change exporter side.

Michael Nguyen: I can pull DB diffs. Which table? `audit_export_configs`?

Benji Okafor: yep. For acme: old row had `enabled=NULL` (we never stored it, defaulted true in service). new row is `enabled=false`.

Ethan Park: wait what. migration script set it?

Michael Nguyen: looking. migration reads legacy json blob -> marshals into new model. if bool missing, Go default is false. if we then persist `enabled=false`, we just disabled them. :facepalm:

Kaitlyn Nguyen: that explains “RBAC didn’t change but it’s off”. we should treat missing as enabled=true for legacy configs.

Amara Diallo: +1. Also on the API side, does PATCH without `enabled` overwrite it? might be doing full replace.

Benji Okafor: it’s PUT-style “updateConfig” that rewrites the whole object from request. But this particular flip happened in background migration job, not user call.

Chloe Martin: Console is showing “Disabled” and CTA to re-enable. Customer thinks we turned it off. We need a fix + cleanup script.

Ethan Park: can we confirm scope? how many legacy configs have `enabled` unset?

Michael Nguyen: quick query in prod snapshot:
```
select count(*) from audit_export_configs where enabled is null;
-- 14
```
looks like 14 configs across 9 tenants. most are beta.

Diego Santos: exporter behavior: it only polls configs and stops processing if `enabled=false`. so this is a hard stop.

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: exporter lag alarms spiked for two tenants overnight then went “green” because throughput dropped to zero (since it stopped). Not ideal. We should add a guard to alert on “disabled unexpectedly” or “config disabled by migration”.

Benji Okafor: agree. For immediate mitigation we can manually flip those 14 back to enabled true.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: careful w/ auditability. we should emit an audit event for the remediation (actor=system or operator). also: don’t make it look like the customer toggled.

Ethan Park: plan:
1) hotfix control-plane to treat missing enabled as true during migration + during decode of legacy blob
2) one-time backfill for rows where we set enabled=false but legacy had missing
3) add a safeguard in migration: do not persist enabled=false unless legacy explicitly disabled

Amara Diallo: also add validation: if destination credentials missing, we shouldn’t auto-disable; we should surface “invalid config” separately.

Michael Nguyen: I found the exact bug. In `migrateLegacyConfig()`:
```
var cfg ExportConfig
json.Unmarshal(legacy, &cfg) // cfg.Enabled defaults false
newRow.Enabled = cfg.Enabled
```
Need pointer bool `*bool` or custom default.

Benji Okafor: yep. simplest is:
- change struct field to `Enabled *bool` for legacy decode
- if nil => true
- if false => false

Ethan Park: do it. please post PR link here.

Benji Okafor: opened hotfix PR: https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood/pull/28847
Summary:
- treat `enabled` missing as true for legacy decode
- migration writes `enabled=true` when legacy missing
- adds metrics counter `audit_export_config_migration_enabled_defaulted_total`

Connor O'Brien: jumping in. we can get this into today’s patch train if we keep it minimal. what’s the rollout risk?

Benji Okafor: low, scoped to migration path + legacy decode. no API surface change. includes unit tests + a small integration test.

Connor O'Brien: ok. once merged I’ll queue release and we’ll run a backfill job. pls provide a SQL snippet + safety check.

Michael Nguyen: backfill suggestion (safe-ish):
1) identify rows updated by migration window where `enabled=false` and `updated_by='migration/audit-export-v2'` (we set that tag)
2) set to true
Example:
```
update audit_export_configs
set enabled=true,
    updated_at=now(),
    updated_by='ops/hotfix-28847'
where enabled=false
  and updated_by='migration/audit-export-v2'
  and created_at < '2025-02-10'
;
```
We should also dump affected tenant_ids first.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 but please don’t use created_at heuristic if we can avoid it. can we filter on a migration batch id?

Michael Nguyen: we have `migration_version='2025-02-13-audit-export-v2'` on those. Better:
```
where enabled=false and migration_version='2025-02-13-audit-export-v2'
```

Chloe Martin: once we flip them back, Console will show enabled again automatically. Do we need to send proactive note to CSMs?

Ethan Park: yes. I’ll ping Miguel/Liam once we confirm the list.

Sean Gallagher: also, for the two impacted customers: can someone confirm exporter resumes after enabling? might be stuck on checkpoints?

Diego Santos: exporter should resume, but if downstream had 401/403 and we auto-paused earlier, that’s separate. This bug is pure config flag.

Benji Okafor: PR is ready for review. @Ethan @Michael please take a look. :pray:

Ethan Park: reviewing now.

Ethan Park: left minor comments (naming + add test for “explicit false remains false”). otherwise looks good.

Benji Okafor: pushed fix + added test.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, please drop the exact backfill query and tenant list in #eng-releases (we need for change record).

Michael Nguyen: tenant list from prod:
- acme-banking-prod
- helio-health-prod
- quantiva-eu-staging
- northstar-insurance-poc
(+5 others internal)
I’ll send full ids to Connor privately.

Sean Gallagher: can we add a temporary alert: “exporter has zero active configs but feature flag on”? that would’ve caught this.

Ethan Park: good idea. Let’s track as follow-up; not blocking hotfix.

DeployBot: PR #28847 merged into main.

Connor O'Brien: queued patch release `control-plane-2025.02.16.3`. ETA ~45m. After deploy, we’ll run backfill + verify status endpoint.

Benji Okafor: thank you. I’ll stay online to validate.

Chloe Martin: once backfill done, I’ll spot check Console for acme + helio and update support ticket.

Ethan Park: postmortem-ish note: migration should never change runtime behavior without an explicit mapping. we need a checklist item: “missing bools default true/false?”

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. also please ensure the audit event emitted by migration includes `actor.type=system` and not an end-user. (we’ve had customers ask “who disabled this”).

Benji Okafor: yep, in PR I added `updated_by='migration/audit-export-v2'` consistently + audit log event `audit.export_config.migrated` actor=system.

Sean Gallagher: once fixed, I’ll adjust the dashboard panel that went green because throughput dropped. that’s super misleading.

Connor O'Brien: patch deployed. proceed with backfill when ready.

Michael Nguyen: running backfill now (read-only verify first):
```
select tenant_id, id, enabled, updated_by, migration_version
from audit_export_configs
where enabled=false and migration_version='2025-02-13-audit-export-v2';
```
14 rows. executing update.

Michael Nguyen: update done. re-query shows enabled=true for all 14. will monitor.

Diego Santos: exporter side: seeing configs picked up again in logs for acme + helio. lag decreasing.

Chloe Martin: Console reflects enabled for acme. closing loop with Support.

Ethan Park: thanks all. Connor can you link this thread in the release notes? and Benji pls add a small follow-up task to harden migrations.

Benji Okafor: will do. creating Linear follow-up + adding checklist to migration template.

source.messages:
Priya Natarajan: ok last thread before we call beta go/no-go today. pls drop *any* last-minute review questions/unknowns here (security, audit, SRE thresholds, UX copy). I’ll assign owners + due times.

Ethan Park: q1: what’s our explicit behavior when telemetry is missing/stale during gate eval? I remember “fail-closed unless manual override” but want it written + reflected in API responses.

Jordan Lee: +1. policy engine currently treats “unknown” as FAIL for automated promote, but doesn’t auto-abort. it sets gate_status=unknown and blocks promotion unless `force_promote`.

Priya Natarajan: owner? Jordan can you summarize final semantics in 3 bullets + where it’s implemented (policy engine vs control plane)? need for release notes.

Jordan Lee: yup, I’ll post by 11:30.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: security q: RBAC for force-promote/hold/abort. do we have a distinct permission or is it bundled under “manage_rollouts”? also: are customers ever able to force-promote in beta?

Ethan Park: control plane has `dedicated.rollouts.promote` and `dedicated.rollouts.abort`. force_promote/hold is gated behind `dedicated.rollouts.admin_override` (admin-only). customer admins won’t have that in beta.

Priya Natarajan: Kaitlyn can you sanity-check the permission names + confirm the customer role mapping is correct? also confirm audit event includes “override=true”.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: yes. I’ll review the perms + role mapping doc and respond here by noon.

Rafael Mendes: SRE q: default thresholds for beta design partners. do we have a single set or tiered by pool size / region? and do we have minimum sample size rules before a gate can pass?

Sean Gallagher: also: do we block promotion if there’s active incident / elevated error budget burn on the pool? I don’t want us promoting into an ongoing fire.

Jordan Lee: we have min sample size check for latency/error gates: require N>=200 requests in window (configurable). If below, gate=unknown (i.e., blocks auto promote).

Sergio Costa: thresholds in dashboards are currently the “standard dedicated” ones: error_rate < 0.5% (5m), p95 < baseline*1.15, saturation < 85% avg, OOM_rate < 0.05% (15m). I’m not sure we finalized the “small pool” adjustments.

Vanessa Ortiz: small pools are the tricky part: canary stage on a 4-GPU reservation starves effective capacity and spikes queue depth/429s. we added validation “min 2 replicas per stage” but % splits can still behave weird depending on batching.

Priya Natarajan: action: Rafael + Vanessa can you propose beta-safe defaults for (a) minimum pool size to allow 1% canary (b) stage set for small pools (maybe replica partition only)? need by EOD for go/no-go notes.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll draft recommendations and send for quick approval.

Vanessa Ortiz: same, will include concrete numbers + “reject plan” validation copy.

Chloe Martin: UX q: copy for “unknown” gates. right now console says “Gate failed” when gate_status=unknown. we should distinguish “insufficient data” vs “failed”. can we align on exact wording?

Kenji Nakamura: +1. we also need text for hold state: “Held by operator” vs “Paused” and whether customer sees the “reason” field.

Priya Natarajan: Kenji + Chloe can you propose final strings for: Gate PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN, rollout state RUNNING/HELD/ABORTED/COMPLETED, and “Force promote” warning modal text. please include customer-facing vs internal admin-facing.

Kenji Nakamura: on it. will drop a mini table.

Chloe Martin: I’ll coordinate w Kenji; we can patch quickly before beta flag.

Logan Wright: telemetry q: we’re tagging stage_id in metrics/traces. are we confident cardinality is bounded? last week we saw a regression from stage tags.

Allison Grant: we have allowlist for labels + stage_id is hashed + limited to 10 active rollout_plans per workspace (enforced at control plane). but I want to confirm dashboards/alerts only use stage_name + stage_index, not raw stage_id.

Logan Wright: policy engine queries use `rollout_stage_index` and `pool_id`, not raw stage_id. traces include stage_id in span attributes though (not as a metric label).

Priya Natarajan: Allison can you confirm what’s in metrics labels vs trace attrs and that it passes observability standards? We can’t ship another cardinality incident.

Allison Grant: yes, I’ll verify against the allowlist + PR-28644 changes and report by 12:30.

Mina Haddad: routing q: rollback semantics for streaming requests. if we abort mid-stage, do in-flight streams keep pinned to canary partition? I think yes, but want to confirm it’s documented + expected.

Ethan Park: current behavior: session/stream is pinned at request admission to a partition (stage). abort/promotion only affects new admissions. there is no mid-stream reroute.

Rafael Mendes: SRE follow-up: does abort also immediately reset weights to previous stable stage across regions? any propagation delay? we need to say what “immediate” means.

Mina Haddad: routing propagation is via config push; p50 ~5s, p99 <30s. during that time, new admissions might still go to canary in some regions. we added “abort drains new traffic” flag to try to force-sticky to stable, but it still depends on cache.

Priya Natarajan: Mina can you write the exact rollback semantics + propagation timings we should communicate to beta customers + oncall? include “what to expect” and limitations.

Mina Haddad: yep will do.

Connor O'Brien: release q: are we done w migrations/backfill? I need a crisp statement for release train: “safe to enable flag; disable plan exists.” also: do we have idempotency keys required for promote/abort calls from console?

Benji Okafor: migrations are additive; backfill populates empty rollout_plans for existing pools only when feature flag is enabled (so no global backfill). promote/abort endpoints require `Idempotency-Key` header; console sends it.

DeployBot: PRD-DeployBot APP  •  Reminder: feature flag name in prod is `dedicated_staged_rollouts_beta`. Current allowlist: 0 accounts. Use `ffctl allowlist add --account <id>`.

Connor O'Brien: thx. Benji can you paste the disable/rollback steps for control plane + routing? I want it in the checklist.

Benji Okafor: sure. tl;dr: flip ff off; control plane stops evaluating gates; routing ignores rollout_plan and routes to stable config. no data loss. I’ll send exact commands in thread.

Irene Choi: infra q: do we have a hard rule on minimum headroom per stage? for beta we should avoid fractional partitions that leave no spare capacity for node drains / GPU health issues.

Vanessa Ortiz: guidelines draft says: keep >=1 GPU headroom in stable partition at all times for pools <=16 GPUs; for larger pools maintain >=10% headroom and avoid canary <2 GPUs per region.

Priya Natarajan: Irene + Vanessa please confirm final “min headroom” line that will be enforced in validation vs only guidance. need to match what API rejects.

Irene Choi: will do. I think we only enforce min 2 replicas per stage (per region) + saturation guardrails; headroom is guidance but we can tighten for beta.

Sean Gallagher: audit/logging q: do we include the “reason” field for promote/abort/force-promote and actor identity (user vs service) in audit events? and are these exportable to SIEM yet?

Ethan Park: audit payload includes actor_id, actor_type, pool_id, rollout_plan_id, from_stage, to_stage, action, reason (optional but strongly recommended), override flag, and request_id. export pipeline issue is in INT-1951; patch is in flight.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: please make reason required for admin_override actions. optional for normal promote/abort is fine.

Priya Natarajan: Ethan can we enforce “reason required” server-side for force-promote/hold? if so, do it before beta.

Ethan Park: yes, easy validation. I’ll file + patch today.

Jada Williams: console backend q: who can see gate evidence details? customers will see pass/fail + some metrics; admins see full query + links. confirm this is ok for security/compliance.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: from security standpoint, showing the metric values is ok; do not expose raw query strings that reveal internal metric namespaces unless we’ve reviewed. keep queries admin-only.

Priya Natarajan: Jada can you confirm response schema matches that (queries redacted unless admin role)?

Jada Williams: yes. currently `evidence.query` is omitted unless `is_admin=true` from authz context.

Sergio Costa: last Q: alert routing. if gate fails, do we page? currently alerts are ticket-only for beta, paging only if error rate is high outside rollout context.

Rafael Mendes: +1, gate failures should create an oncall signal but not page by default. paging is too noisy.

Priya Natarajan: Rafael + Sergio: please confirm final alert policy for beta (page vs ticket) + destinations (#dedicated-oncall vs #eng-platform-oncall). need before go/no-go meeting.

Rafael Mendes: will do.

Priya Natarajan: recap owners:
- Jordan: missing/stale telemetry semantics summary (11:30)
- Kaitlyn: RBAC + role mapping + audit override flag + reason-required guidance (12:00)
- Allison: metrics labels vs trace attrs + cardinality signoff (12:30)
- Kenji/Chloe: final UX copy table (EOD, ideally before 3pm)
- Mina: rollback/streaming + propagation timing writeup (2pm)
- Rafael/Vanessa/Irene: small pool defaults + min headroom/validation rules (EOD)
- Rafael/Sergio: alerting policy for gate failures (2pm)
- Ethan: enforce reason required for admin overrides + confirm audit payload fields (today)

Priya Natarajan: if you have more Qs drop them now. otherwise I’ll pull these into the go/no-go doc.

Connor O'Brien: +1, thanks. once we have those answers I can finalize the beta enablement checklist + comms.

Kenji Nakamura: FYI I’ll also add a one-liner tooltip text for “Force promote” to make it clear it may violate SLOs + is audited.

Priya Natarajan: perfect.
